manifest.title: A2 Dutch — Multimodal everyday path (nl-NL)
manifest.summary: Nine thematic units (72 lessons) rotating input, pattern practice, extended listening/reading, writing/speaking studios, real-world tasks, and culture capsules. Aligned to CEFR A2 with explicit 8-phase lesson spine.
manifest.exam_alignment_notes: Themes echo common civic-integration domains using original scenarios only — not exam-specific wording.
manifest.source_notes: Author: curriculum generator script; original Dutch samples; pedagogy from a2-multimodal-curriculum-design.md; 2026-03-25.

units.title: Early A2 — People & daily rhythm
units.summary: Introduce yourself and others, talk about routines, time, and simple plans in Dutch daily life.
objectives_can_do: Exchange basic personal information and describe family or household in simple terms
Talk about typical weekday and weekend routines using present tense
Ask and answer about time, appointments, and simple future plans
Use polite forms (u/jij) appropriately in short service-style exchanges
Understand short spoken or written messages about schedules if vocabulary is familiar
React appropriately to simple social questions about habits and preferences
units.integration_scripts_summary: Neighbour and rooster chat; kennismaken in informal Dutch.
grammar_focus: present tense (weak verbs, common irregulars: zijn, hebben, gaan)
word order in main clauses and yes/no questions
separable verbs in present (opstaan, meenemen)
expressions with te + infinitive (te laat zijn)

units.title: Early A2 — Food & shopping
units.summary: Shop for food, ask about products, pay, and express preferences and dietary needs.
objectives_can_do: Ask for items and quantities in a supermarket, market, or bakery
Understand prices, offers, and simple labels on packaging
Say what you like or avoid (allergies, vegetarian) in simple sentences
Handle basic checkout phrases and payment methods
Read a short shopping list or recipe instruction at A2 level
Make a simple complaint or request (e.g. wrong product) politely
units.integration_scripts_summary: Albert Heijn-style checkout, dietary phrases, paying by card.
grammar_focus: modal verbs: willen, kunnen, mogen (polite requests)
measure words and containers (een kilo, een pak, een fles)
comparatives (goedkoper, lekkerder) in short opinions
imperatives for shopping (Geeft u mij …, Mag ik …)

units.title: Early A2 — Housing & neighbourhood
units.summary: Describe your home, talk to neighbours, and handle simple housing and building issues.
objectives_can_do: Describe rooms, furniture, and rental basics (huur, borg) in simple Dutch
Ask a neighbour or landlord for help with a small problem
Understand short notices about building rules or maintenance
Give simple directions inside a flat or small building
Fill in basic fields on a housing-related form with support
Express likes/dislikes about living area and facilities
units.integration_scripts_summary: Landlord and buren messages; housing repairs vocabulary.
grammar_focus: prepositions of place (naast, boven, achter, tussen)
er + locative (er is, er zijn)
possessive pronouns (mijn, jouw, zijn/haar, ons)
simple passive-style signs (niet roken, verboden toegang)

units.title: Mid A2 — Transport & city
units.summary: Use public transport, ask for directions, and deal with delays and appointments in town.
objectives_can_do: Buy or check-in for train, bus, tram, or metro travel in the Netherlands
Ask for and understand simple directions and changes
Explain you are late or early and reschedule in short messages
Read departure boards and simple travel apps with support
Describe a city trip or commute in a few connected sentences
Handle a simple ticket or gate problem with help phrases
units.integration_scripts_summary: OV desk, delays, and simple rescheduling phrases.
grammar_focus: motion verbs + preposition (naar, met, van … naar …)
connectors: eerst, daarna, totdat (simple sequences)
future with gaan + infinitive for plans
time clauses with als, wanneer (basic)

units.title: Mid A2 — Health & body
units.summary: Describe symptoms, use GP and pharmacy phrases, and follow simple health advice.
objectives_can_do: Name common body parts and mild symptoms in Dutch
Make a short appointment request with a huisartsenpraktijk
Understand simple instructions from a GP assistant or pharmacist
Ask for over-the-counter medicine with basic constraints
Describe pain level and duration in simple phrases
React appropriately to advice (rust, water drinken, paracetamol)
units.integration_scripts_summary: Huisarts phone scripts; pharmacy and symptom chunks.
grammar_focus: reflexive verbs (zich voelen, zich wassen)
modal zullen for soft advice (Je zult moeten …) — recognition
past tense recognition (ik had pijn) for narratives
polite requests with zou … kunnen

units.title: Mid A2 — Health & doctor visits
units.summary: Describe symptoms, talk with a GP or pharmacy, make appointments, and follow simple instructions (moeten/mogen) — calm, practical Dutch.
objectives_can_do: Name common symptoms and say how long you have felt unwell
Follow a short doctor or pharmacy conversation and ask for repetition
Make or change a simple appointment by phone or message
Understand basic medicine instructions (how often, before/after food)
Use moeten/mogen/kunnen in short advice sentences
Stay polite and clear under mild stress at spreekuur
units.integration_scripts_summary: GP intake, pharmacy pickup, appointment desk — aligned to Stage 6 schema module a2-m06-health-doctor (eleven lessons).
grammar_focus: symptom patterns: ik heb … / ik voel me … / het doet pijn
doctor questions: sinds wanneer, hoe lang al, waar
light perfectum: slecht geslapen, koorts gehad
modals in advice: u moet, u mag, u kunt
clarifying: kunt u herhalen, langzamer praten, ik begrijp het niet

units.title: Late A2 — Admin & services
units.summary: Handle gemeente, bank, and post situations with simple scripted Dutch.
objectives_can_do: Ask what documents you need for a simple gemeente appointment
Understand basic instructions at a service desk (nummer trekken, wachten)
Request a duplicate card or change of address in simple phrases
Read short official letters or emails with familiar vocabulary
Make a short phone script for identification and purpose
Ask someone to repeat or speak more slowly in Dutch
units.integration_scripts_summary: Gemeente desk and DigiD vocabulary; ID appointment flow.
grammar_focus: conditional polite forms (Ik zou graag … willen)
passive-like bureaucratic phrases (wordt verwerkt) — recognition
relative clauses with die/dat (very short)
numbers and dates in official contexts

units.title: Late A2 — Social & leisure
units.summary: Invite friends, talk about hobbies, and share simple opinions about films, sport, and free time.
objectives_can_do: Invite, accept, or decline invitations with reasons
Talk about hobbies and weekend plans in connected sentences
Give simple likes/dislikes about music, sport, or TV
Ask follow-up questions in informal chat (En jij?)
Understand short event posters or social media posts
Suggest a time and place for meeting friends
units.integration_scripts_summary: Invitations, preferences, and casual plans with friends.
grammar_focus: want, omdat for reasons (simple clauses)
graag, liever for preferences
diminutives in informal speech (biertje, filmpje) — recognition/production
tag questions and fillers (toch?, hè?) — careful use

units.title: Late A2 — Culture & integration context
units.summary: Explore Dutch holidays, school touchpoints, etiquette, and institutions at A2 depth.
objectives_can_do: Name major Dutch holidays and what people typically do
Understand basics of primary school routines and parent contact (rough picture)
Adjust tone for direct but polite Dutch communication in familiar settings
Describe one or two civic habits (cycling, calendars, punctuality) simply
Understand short texts about traditions without exam-specific copying
Ask respectful questions about local customs
units.integration_scripts_summary: Holidays, schools, and polite directness — chunk-based Dutch.
grammar_focus: impersonal constructions (men …, je … as 'one')
present tense narratives about customs
fixed expressions for holidays and greetings
contrast: formal/informal in public settings

catalog.title: Listen & read · Catch real Dutch in short, friendly chats
catalog.description: Catch real Dutch in short, friendly chats · A2 listen & read in “People & daily rhythm”: Dutch you can use the same week, guided steps, and a short check at the end.

pedagogy.objective: By the end of this lesson, you can understand short input about people & daily rhythm, notice useful chunks, and answer gist/detail questions
can_do_outcomes: Understand gist and one detail in a short dialogue about daily rhythm
Recognise present-tense chunks about workdays and weekends
Say or write one sentence about your own typical day

grammar_points.point: present tense (weak verbs, common irregulars: zijn, hebben, gaan)
examples_nl: Ik werk vier dagen op kantoor.
We hebben vanavond een afspraak bij vrienden.
examples_en: I work four days in the office.
We have an appointment with friends tonight.

grammar_points.point: word order in main clauses and yes/no questions
examples_nl: Ga je morgen naar je cursus?
Hoe laat sta jij meestal op?
examples_en: Are you going to your class tomorrow?
What time do you usually get up?

grammar_points.point: separable verbs in present (opstaan, meenemen)
examples_nl: Ik ruim de keuken op na het eten.
Zet je de wekker vroeg voor je rooster?
examples_en: I tidy the kitchen after dinner.
Do you set the alarm early for your schedule?

micro_outcomes: I can recognise key words about people & daily rhythm in short Dutch texts or audio.
I can copy at least one useful chunk into a sentence about myself.
I can finish most guided tasks in the lesson without peeking at translations on the second try.
I can pass the quick self-check at the end of the lesson.
I know what to revise tomorrow in two minutes or less.

steps.activity:
Welcome! This lesson is built in small steps so you always know where you are.

**What you'll be able to do:** Understand short input about people & daily rhythm, notice useful chunks, and answer gist/detail questions

**Grammar thread today:** Present tense (regular + zijn/hebben/gaan) — you'll get explanations, examples, and production tasks in **Language focus** and **Guided practice**.

Take your time — you can repeat any step. Try to say at least one Dutch sentence out loud today.
steps.teacher_notes: Keep under ~1 min; reassure anxious learners.

steps.activity:
Let's wake up vocabulary about **People & daily rhythm**.

Words you might use today:
• **kennismaken**
• **collega**
• **familie**
• **rooster**
• **weekend**
• **afspraak**
• **punctual**
• **ochtendritueel**

**Your turn (1–2 short sentences, Dutch or mixed):**
**Wat deed je gisteren?** — *What did you do yesterday?*

If you only know a few words, that's fine — start there.
steps.teacher_notes: Accept partial Dutch; encourage gestures.
steps.example_response: Gisteren werkte ik tot vijf uur. Daarna fietste ik naar huis en kookte ik een eenvoudige maaltijd. 's Avonds las ik nog even en ging ik op tijd naar bed.

illustration.alt: Diagram of a day: morning (coffee), afternoon (work or study), evening (rest)

steps.activity:
**Listen & read** this short conversation. Read twice: first for gist, then for details.

**Sam:** Hoi! Ik heet Sam. Ik kom uit Berlijn en ik woon nu in Utrecht.

**Mila:** Leuk! Ik ben Mila. Ik werk in een café en ik studeer 's avonds Nederlands.

**Sam:** Wat doe jij meestal in het weekend?

**Mila:** Vaak fiets ik naar vrienden. Soms blijf ik thuis en kijk ik een serie.

**Sam:** Ik ga graag naar de markt op zaterdag.

**Mila:** Gezellig! Tot gauw, dan drinken we koffie.

**Gist check:** Who are the people, and what is the situation?
**Detail check:** Note one time phrase or place you recognise.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — present tense (A2 core)**

**Rule (English):** Dutch present often looks like English *I work / we have*, but irregulars (*zijn, hebben, gaan*) and *jij* forms need attention.

**Patterns to copy (Dutch):**
• *Ik werk vandaag thuis.* · *Wij hebben een afspraak.* · *Ga je mee naar de supermarkt?*

**Produce (do now):**
1. Change *Ik werk* → *Wij werken* with the same time phrase.
2. Ask one yes/no question with the verb first: *Heb je …?* or *Ga je …?*

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — g / ch:** practise *goed — licht — dag — nacht* with TTS; back *g* vs softer *ch*.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**present tense (weak verbs, common irregulars: zijn, hebben, gaan)**
• NL: Ik werk vier dagen op kantoor. / We hebben vanavond een afspraak bij vrienden.
• EN: I work four days in the office. / We have an appointment with friends tonight.

**word order in main clauses and yes/no questions**
• NL: Ga je morgen naar je cursus? / Hoe laat sta jij meestal op?
• EN: Are you going to your class tomorrow? / What time do you usually get up?

**separable verbs in present (opstaan, meenemen)**
• NL: Ik ruim de keuken op na het eten. / Zet je de wekker vroeg voor je rooster?
• EN: I tidy the kitchen after dinner. / Do you set the alarm early for your schedule?

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.


**Culture tip:** Many Dutch people plan social visits in advance; spontaneous dropping by is less common than in some cultures.
*How does this compare with where you live? One sentence in your own words.*
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Match** (say the pairs out loud)
• **kennismaken** ↔ a word you connect with daily life
• **collega** ↔ a place or thing from this unit

**2 — Gap-fill**
Complete: Ik _____ om acht uur. *(opstaan → stem: Ik **sta** … **op**)*

**3 — Mini dialogue**
**A:** Hoe laat ben je klaar? **B:** Om half zes, denk ik.

**4 — Find the odd one**
Which word fits *least* with this unit: **familie**, **broodje**, **trein**? Why?

**5 — True / false**
Say **waar** or **niet waar**: "In Dutch, the verb often moves when you ask a yes/no question."

**6 — Your sentence**
Write one true sentence about yourself using **kennismaken** or **collega**.

**7 — Transform**
Make this informal line a bit more formal: "Kun je me helpen?" → start with **Kunt u …**

**8 — Quick translation check**
"Ik werk vier dagen op kantoor." — what does it mean in English (your own words)?
---

**All four skills — finish this lesson with these four micro-tasks**

🎧 **Listening:** Use TTS on this Dutch line (slow, then closer to natural): *Sam: Hoi! Ik heet Sam. Ik kom uit Berlijn en ik woon nu in Utrecht.*

📖 **Reading:** Read silently, then say the **main idea in one English sentence**: *Sam: Hoi! Ik heet Sam. Ik kom uit Berlijn en ik woon nu in Utrecht.*

✍️ **Writing:** Write **one new Dutch sentence** that uses **kennismaken** and fits *Present tense (regular + zijn/hebben/gaan)*.

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*
steps.teacher_notes: Mix modalities; at least 6 tasks — all concrete above.

steps.activity:
**Phrase bank (use freely)**
• kennismaken
• collega
• familie
• rooster
• weekend
• afspraak
• punctual
• ochtendritueel
• slaap
• ontbijt
• pendelen
• agenda

**Your task:** Write or say **4–6 short sentences** in Dutch about this unit theme.
Rules: include **at least one question** and one connector (**en**, **maar**, or **want**).

**Example starter (you can change it):**
Vandaag … Ik wil … **want** … **En jij?**
steps.teacher_notes: Encourage intelligibility over perfection.

steps.activity:
**Quick self-check**

Try each question, then tap **Check answer**. The correct answer stays hidden until you submit.

**Tip:** Say Dutch options out loud if that helps.

**Afterwards:** If three or more felt easy, open **Quiz** for a short recap. If not, go back to **Guided practice** once more.
steps.teacher_notes: Rendered with interactive UI when interaction is present.

items.prompt: Pick the best reply when someone says **Hoe gaat het?**
items.feedbackCorrect: Nice — this is a natural, friendly reply.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Tot ziens** is goodbye; **Graag gedaan** fits after *dank je*. After *Hoe gaat het?* people often say how they feel, e.g. **Goed, dank je!**

items.prompt: Complete: **Ik ___ Nederlands sinds januari.**
items.hint: Present tense of *leren* (to learn), 1st person singular.
items.feedbackCorrect: Yes — *Ik leer Nederlands sinds januari.*
items.feedbackIncorrect: The present of *leren* (ik-form) is **leer**.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.
items.feedbackIncorrect: This is **true**: many yes/no questions start with the verb.

items.prompt: Say or write **one new sentence** using **kennismaken**.
items.exampleAnswer: Example: *Ik wil graag kennismaken met mijn nieuwe buren.* (adjust to your situation.)

steps.activity:
**Nice work.**

Next in this unit: **Listen & read · Who said what? Listen, read, respond** — we keep building **People & daily rhythm** with a new focus.

**2-minute revision:** skim the phrase bank from step 6 once more.

When you're ready, open **Quiz** for a compact recap — or **Flashcards** for the lemmas.

**Dutch culture insight**

Many Dutch people **plan social visits** a day or more ahead; dropping by unannounced is less common than in some cultures. A short app (*“Ben je vanavond thuis?”*) is normal and friendly.

**Phrases to steal (Dutch → English)**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Ben je vanavond thuis?* | Are you home tonight? |
| *Zullen we een afspraak maken?* | Shall we set a time? |
| *Sorry, ik ben een beetje te laat.* | Sorry, I'm a little late. |

**Mini dialogue**

**A:** Hé, heb je zin om zaterdag koffie te drinken?
**B:** Ja, leuk! Hoe laat ongeveer?
**A:** Rond elf uur bij het station?
**B:** Prima, ik stuur je morgen een appje.

**Say or write:** Invite someone for coffee using *Zullen we …* or *Heb je zin om …*.

*Optional reflection:* In one English sentence, what feels similar or different where you live?
steps.teacher_notes: Link to next lesson id in CMS when wired.

quiz_ideas: Self-check: choose the best reply to "Hoe gaat het?" (see step 7 in the lesson).
Gap-fill idea: write a sentence with **kennismaken** in the present tense.
True/false: in Dutch yes/no questions the verb often comes before the subject.
Say one new polite request starting with **Kunt u …** or **Mag ik …**.
Paraphrase one Dutch line from the lesson input in your own English.
assessment.success_criteria: You can complete the step-7 self-check comfortably and produce at least one clear Dutch sentence about the lesson theme (spoken or written).

content_refs.notes: Slow audio assumed for lines marked *slow* in builder CMS.

steps.activity:
**Listen & read** this short conversation. Read twice: first for gist, then for details.

**Docent:** Goedemorgen. Wie wil eerst iets over zichzelf vertellen?

**Jin:** Ik werk vier dagen op kantoor. Op woensdag werk ik thuis.

**Docent:** En hoe laat begin je meestal?

**Jin:** Om half negen. Ik drink eerst koffie en daarna check ik mijn e-mail.

**Docent:** Dank je. Dat is een duidelijk ritme.

**Gist check:** Who are the people, and what is the situation?
**Detail check:** Note one time phrase or place you recognise.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — statement vs question order**

**Rule:** In neutral statements the verb sits **after** the subject (*Ik ga morgen*). In yes/no questions the verb often moves **before** the subject (*Ga je morgen?*).

**Patterns:**
• *Ik bel je straks.* · *Bel je me straks?* · *We eten om zeven.* · *Eten jullie om zeven?*

**Produce:**
1. Turn *We gaan naar het station* into a question (verb first).
2. Turn *Kom je vanavond?* into a calm statement (subject + verb).

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — ui / eu / ij:** round lips for *huis — leeuw*; *mijn* is one smooth glide.

**Pronunciation spotlight — g / ch**

Listen to the TTS, then repeat slowly: **goed — groot — licht — nacht**.
Aim for a clear back-of-mouth **g** in *goed/groot* and a softer **ch** in *licht/nacht*.



**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**present tense (weak verbs, common irregulars: zijn, hebben, gaan)**
• NL: We hebben vanavond een afspraak bij vrienden. / Ik werk vier dagen op kantoor.
• EN: We have an appointment with friends tonight. / I work four days in the office.

**word order in main clauses and yes/no questions**
• NL: Hoe laat sta jij meestal op? / Ga je morgen naar je cursus?
• EN: What time do you usually get up? / Are you going to your class tomorrow?

**separable verbs in present (opstaan, meenemen)**
• NL: Zet je de wekker vroeg voor je rooster? / Ik ruim de keuken op na het eten.
• EN: Do you set the alarm early for your schedule? / I tidy the kitchen after dinner.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

**4 — Find the odd one**
Which word fits *least* with this unit: **afspraak**, **broodje**, **trein**? Why?

**5 — True / false**
Say **waar** or **niet waar**: "In Dutch, the verb often moves when you ask a yes/no question."

**6 — Your sentence**

**7 — Transform**
Make this informal line a bit more formal: "Kun je me helpen?" → start with **Kunt u …**

**8 — Quick translation check**

---

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*

items.prompt: Pick the best reply when someone says **Hoe gaat het?**
items.feedbackCorrect: Nice — this is a natural, friendly reply.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Tot ziens** is goodbye; **Graag gedaan** fits after *dank je*. After *Hoe gaat het?* people often say how they feel, e.g. **Goed, dank je!**

items.prompt: Complete: **Ik ___ Nederlands sinds januari.**
items.hint: Present tense of *leren* (to learn), 1st person singular.
items.feedbackCorrect: Yes — *Ik leer Nederlands sinds januari.*
items.feedbackIncorrect: The present of *leren* (ik-form) is **leer**.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.
items.feedbackIncorrect: This is **true**: many yes/no questions start with the verb.

items.prompt: Say or write **one new sentence** using **rooster**.
items.exampleAnswer: Example: *Ik wil graag rooster met mijn nieuwe buren.* (adjust to your situation.)

steps.activity:
**Nice work.**

Next in this unit: **Patterns & drills · Word order without the headache** — we keep building **People & daily rhythm** with a new focus.

**2-minute revision:** skim the phrase bank from step 6 once more.

When you're ready, open **Quiz** for a compact recap — or **Flashcards** for the lemmas.

**Dutch culture insight**

**Agenda culture** shows up in everyday language: *Hoe laat*, *afspraak*, and *in de pauze* pop up in offices and schools. Being a few minutes late usually gets a quick *Sorry, vertraging* rather than a long excuse.

**Phrases to steal (Dutch → English)**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Ben je vanavond thuis?* | Are you home tonight? |
| *Zullen we een afspraak maken?* | Shall we set a time? |
| *Sorry, ik ben een beetje te laat.* | Sorry, I'm a little late. |

**Mini dialogue**

**A:** Hé, heb je zin om zaterdag koffie te drinken?
**B:** Ja, leuk! Hoe laat ongeveer?
**A:** Rond elf uur bij het station?
**B:** Prima, ik stuur je morgen een appje.

**Say or write:** Invite someone for coffee using *Zullen we …* or *Heb je zin om …*.

*Optional reflection:* In one English sentence, what feels similar or different where you live?
steps.teacher_notes: Link to next lesson id in CMS when wired.

steps.activity:
Welcome! This lesson is built in small steps so you always know where you are.

**What you'll be able to do:** Produce accurate sentence patterns about people & daily rhythm using controlled substitution and drills

**Grammar thread today:** Separable verbs in present (opstaan, meenemen) — you'll get explanations, examples, and production tasks in **Language focus** and **Guided practice**.

Take your time — you can repeat any step. Try to say at least one Dutch sentence out loud today.
steps.teacher_notes: Keep under ~1 min; reassure anxious learners.

steps.activity:
Let's wake up vocabulary about **People & daily rhythm**.

Words you might use today:
• **punctual**
• **ochtendritueel**
• **slaap**
• **ontbijt**
• **pendelen**
• **agenda**
• **kennismaken**
• **collega**

**Your turn (1–2 short sentences, Dutch or mixed):**

If you only know a few words, that's fine — start there.
steps.teacher_notes: Accept partial Dutch; encourage gestures.

steps.activity:
**Input — patterns**

Below are sentences from this theme. They show word order you can copy.

**present tense (weak verbs, common irregulars: zijn, hebben, gaan)**
• NL: Ik werk vier dagen op kantoor. / We hebben vanavond een afspraak bij vrienden.
• EN: I work four days in the office. / We have an appointment with friends tonight.

**word order in main clauses and yes/no questions**
• NL: Ga je morgen naar je cursus? / Hoe laat sta jij meestal op?
• EN: Are you going to your class tomorrow? / What time do you usually get up?

**separable verbs in present (opstaan, meenemen)**
• NL: Ik ruim de keuken op na het eten. / Zet je de wekker vroeg voor je rooster?
• EN: I tidy the kitchen after dinner. / Do you set the alarm early for your schedule?

Read them aloud once, slowly.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — separable verbs**

**Rule:** Many verbs split: **prefix** goes to the end in present main clauses (*Ik **sta** om zeven **op***).

**Patterns:**
• *Ik ruim de keuken op.* · *Zet je de wekker uit?* · *Bel je me later terug?*

**Produce:**
1. Put *opstaan* in a sentence about tomorrow morning (correct split).
2. Fix: *Ik opsta om zes* → correct order.

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — sch / sj:** *school* vs *sjabloon* — listen twice, then shadow.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**present tense (weak verbs, common irregulars: zijn, hebben, gaan)**
• NL: Ik werk vier dagen op kantoor. / We hebben vanavond een afspraak bij vrienden.
• EN: I work four days in the office. / We have an appointment with friends tonight.

**word order in main clauses and yes/no questions**
• NL: Ga je morgen naar je cursus? / Hoe laat sta jij meestal op?
• EN: Are you going to your class tomorrow? / What time do you usually get up?

**separable verbs in present (opstaan, meenemen)**
• NL: Ik ruim de keuken op na het eten. / Zet je de wekker vroeg voor je rooster?
• EN: I tidy the kitchen after dinner. / Do you set the alarm early for your schedule?

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Match** (say the pairs out loud)
• **punctual** ↔ a word you connect with daily life
• **ochtendritueel** ↔ a place or thing from this unit

**2 — Gap-fill**
Complete: Ik _____ om acht uur. *(opstaan → stem: Ik **sta** … **op**)*

**3 — Mini dialogue**
**A:** Hoe laat ben je klaar? **B:** Om half zes, denk ik.

**4 — Find the odd one**
Which word fits *least* with this unit: **slaap**, **broodje**, **trein**? Why?

**5 — True / false**
Say **waar** or **niet waar**: "In Dutch, the verb often moves when you ask a yes/no question."

**6 — Your sentence**
Write one true sentence about yourself using **punctual** or **ochtendritueel**.

**7 — Transform**
Make this informal line a bit more formal: "Kun je me helpen?" → start with **Kunt u …**

**8 — Quick translation check**
"Ik werk vier dagen op kantoor." — what does it mean in English (your own words)?
---

**All four skills — finish this lesson with these four micro-tasks**

🎧 **Listening:** Use TTS on this Dutch line (slow, then closer to natural): *Ik werk vier dagen op kantoor.*

📖 **Reading:** Read silently, then say the **main idea in one English sentence**: *Ik werk vier dagen op kantoor.*

✍️ **Writing:** Write **one new Dutch sentence** that uses **punctual** and fits *Separable verbs in present (opstaan, meenemen)*.

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*
steps.teacher_notes: Mix modalities; at least 6 tasks — all concrete above.

**Dutch culture insight**

Weekend rhythm often mixes **chores, sport, and gezelligheid**: markets, terraces, or *een stukje fietsen*. Saying *Fijne weekend!* on Friday is standard in shops and at work.

**Phrases to steal (Dutch → English)**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Ben je vanavond thuis?* | Are you home tonight? |
| *Zullen we een afspraak maken?* | Shall we set a time? |
| *Sorry, ik ben een beetje te laat.* | Sorry, I'm a little late. |

**Mini dialogue**

**A:** Hé, heb je zin om zaterdag koffie te drinken?
**B:** Ja, leuk! Hoe laat ongeveer?
**A:** Rond elf uur bij het station?
**B:** Prima, ik stuur je morgen een appje.

**Say or write:** Invite someone for coffee using *Zullen we …* or *Heb je zin om …*.

steps.activity:
**Listening focus**

Play the audio twice if you can (slow speed first is fine).

**Transcript** (read along on the second listen):

Spreker 1: Hoi, ik ben Lisa. Ik woon hier sinds februari.
Spreker 2: Leuk! Ik heet Omar. Werk je in de buurt?
Spreker 1: Ja, ik werk vier dagen op kantoor. Vrijdag werk ik thuis.
Spreker 2: Ah, handig. Wat doe je meestal 's avonds?
Spreker 1: Ik kook, en daarna wandelen met de hond. En jij?
Spreker 2: Ik sport op dinsdag en donderdag. In het weekend slaap ik graag uit.

**While you listen:** underline in your mind who wants what and any numbers you hear.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

illustration.alt: Headphones icon suggesting focused listening before reading the transcript

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — present tense (A2 core)**

**Rule (English):** Dutch present often looks like English *I work / we have*, but irregulars (*zijn, hebben, gaan*) and *jij* forms need attention.

**Patterns to copy (Dutch):**
• *Ik werk vandaag thuis.* · *Wij hebben een afspraak.* · *Ga je mee naar de supermarkt?*

**Produce (do now):**
1. Change *Ik werk* → *Wij werken* with the same time phrase.
2. Ask one yes/no question with the verb first: *Heb je …?* or *Ga je …?*

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — stress:** stress content words: *Ik wil een afspraak maken* (not every syllable equal).

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**present tense (weak verbs, common irregulars: zijn, hebben, gaan)**
• NL: We hebben vanavond een afspraak bij vrienden. / Ik werk vier dagen op kantoor.
• EN: We have an appointment with friends tonight. / I work four days in the office.

**word order in main clauses and yes/no questions**
• NL: Hoe laat sta jij meestal op? / Ga je morgen naar je cursus?
• EN: What time do you usually get up? / Are you going to your class tomorrow?

**separable verbs in present (opstaan, meenemen)**
• NL: Zet je de wekker vroeg voor je rooster? / Ik ruim de keuken op na het eten.
• EN: Do you set the alarm early for your schedule? / I tidy the kitchen after dinner.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Before listening:** guess three words you might hear (write them).

**2 — Gist:** is this about work, family, travel, or shopping?

**3 — Numbers:** write every number or time you hear in the script.

**4 — Who:** who takes which action (choose: A / B / both)?

**5 — Polite or informal:** find one **u**-sentence and one **je/jij**-sentence.

**6 — Paraphrase:** in English, one sentence — what do they want?

**7 — Shadowing:** read one speaker's lines aloud with the transcript.

**8 — New chunk:** pick one phrase to use in your own week.

**Script reminder:**
Spreker 1: Hoi, ik ben Lisa. Ik woon hier sinds februari.
Spreker 2: Leuk! Ik heet Omar. Werk je in de buurt?
Spreker 1: Ja, ik werk vier dagen op kantoor. Vrijdag werk ik thuis.
Spreker 2: Ah, handig. Wat doe je meestal 's avonds?
Spreker 1: Ik kook, en daarna wandelen met de hond. En jij?
Spreker 2: Ik sport op dinsdag en donderdag. In het weekend slaap ik graag uit.
---

**All four skills — finish this lesson with these four micro-tasks**

🎧 **Listening:** Use TTS on this Dutch line (slow, then closer to natural): *Spreker 1: Hoi, ik ben Lisa. Ik woon hier sinds februari.*

📖 **Reading:** Read silently, then say the **main idea in one English sentence**: *Spreker 1: Hoi, ik ben Lisa. Ik woon hier sinds februari.*

✍️ **Writing:** Write **one new Dutch sentence** that uses **ontbijt** and fits *Present tense (regular + zijn/hebben/gaan)*.

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*

steps.activity:
**Model message (Dutch)**

Subject: Vraag over bon

Goedemiddag,

Gisteren heb ik boodschappen gedaan bij uw winkel. Ik mis de bon in mijn tas. Kunt u een kopie mailen?

Met vriendelijke groet,
[jouw naam]

**Notice:** polite opening, short problem, clear request, closing.

**Later you will write:** Schrijf een kort appje (4–5 zinnen) naar een collega: je bent 10 minuten te laat en je neemt koffie mee.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — statement vs question order**

**Rule:** In neutral statements the verb sits **after** the subject (*Ik ga morgen*). In yes/no questions the verb often moves **before** the subject (*Ga je morgen?*).

**Patterns:**
• *Ik bel je straks.* · *Bel je me straks?* · *We eten om zeven.* · *Eten jullie om zeven?*

**Produce:**
1. Turn *We gaan naar het station* into a question (verb first).
2. Turn *Kom je vanavond?* into a calm statement (subject + verb).

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — rhythm:** short chunks: *van de — naar het — met een* — run them together softly.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**present tense (weak verbs, common irregulars: zijn, hebben, gaan)**
• NL: Ik werk vier dagen op kantoor. / We hebben vanavond een afspraak bij vrienden.
• EN: I work four days in the office. / We have an appointment with friends tonight.

**word order in main clauses and yes/no questions**
• NL: Ga je morgen naar je cursus? / Hoe laat sta jij meestal op?
• EN: Are you going to your class tomorrow? / What time do you usually get up?

**separable verbs in present (opstaan, meenemen)**
• NL: Ik ruim de keuken op na het eten. / Zet je de wekker vroeg voor je rooster?
• EN: I tidy the kitchen after dinner. / Do you set the alarm early for your schedule?

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Label the parts:** opening / problem / request / closing in the model email.

**2 — Gap-fill:** Met vriendelijke ______, [naam].

**3 — Politeness:** choose softer Dutch: **Ik wil** → **Ik zou graag willen** in one sentence.

**4 — Combine:** Link with **want**: *Ik bel morgen, want ik ben vandaag druk.*

**5 — Shorten:** remove three words without losing the message.

**6 — Checklist tick:** subject line clear? closing present?

**7 — Your details:** replace [naam] and add one real detail from your life.

**8 — Read aloud:** read your draft slowly.
---

**All four skills — finish this lesson with these four micro-tasks**

🎧 **Listening:** Use TTS on this Dutch line (slow, then closer to natural): *Ik werk vier dagen op kantoor.*

📖 **Reading:** Read silently, then say the **main idea in one English sentence**: *Ik werk vier dagen op kantoor.*

✍️ **Writing:** Write **one new Dutch sentence** that uses **kennismaken** and fits *Main clause vs yes/no question word order*.

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*
steps.teacher_notes: Mix modalities; at least 6 tasks — all concrete above.

items.prompt: Pick the best reply when someone says **Hoe gaat het?**
items.feedbackCorrect: Nice — this is a natural, friendly reply.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Tot ziens** is goodbye; **Graag gedaan** fits after *dank je*. After *Hoe gaat het?* people often say how they feel, e.g. **Goed, dank je!**

items.prompt: Complete: **Ik ___ Nederlands sinds januari.**
items.hint: Present tense of *leren* (to learn), 1st person singular.
items.feedbackCorrect: Yes — *Ik leer Nederlands sinds januari.*
items.feedbackIncorrect: The present of *leren* (ik-form) is **leer**.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.
items.feedbackIncorrect: This is **true**: many yes/no questions start with the verb.

items.prompt: Say or write **one new sentence** using **kennismaken**.
items.exampleAnswer: Example: *Ik wil graag kennismaken met mijn nieuwe buren.* (adjust to your situation.)

steps.activity:
**Nice work.**

Next in this unit: **Real-life task · When manners matter as much as grammar** — we keep building **People & daily rhythm** with a new focus.

**2-minute revision:** skim the phrase bank from step 6 once more.

When you're ready, open **Quiz** for a compact recap — or **Flashcards** for the lemmas.

**Dutch culture insight**

Many Dutch people **plan social visits** a day or more ahead; dropping by unannounced is less common than in some cultures. A short app (*“Ben je vanavond thuis?”*) is normal and friendly.

**Phrases to steal (Dutch → English)**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Ben je vanavond thuis?* | Are you home tonight? |
| *Zullen we een afspraak maken?* | Shall we set a time? |
| *Sorry, ik ben een beetje te laat.* | Sorry, I'm a little late. |

**Mini dialogue**

**A:** Hé, heb je zin om zaterdag koffie te drinken?
**B:** Ja, leuk! Hoe laat ongeveer?
**A:** Rond elf uur bij het station?
**B:** Prima, ik stuur je morgen een appje.

**Say or write:** Invite someone for coffee using *Zullen we …* or *Heb je zin om …*.

*Optional reflection:* In one English sentence, what feels similar or different where you live?

steps.activity:
**Mini situation**

You get a short message that sounds a bit too direct in Dutch. Your job in the next steps is to choose a polite fix.

**Message (Dutch):**
Hoi, je bent steeds te laat op maandag. Fix dit.

**Hi, you're always late on Monday. Fix this.**

We will upgrade the tone without changing the facts.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — separable verbs**

**Rule:** Many verbs split: **prefix** goes to the end in present main clauses (*Ik **sta** om zeven **op***).

**Patterns:**
• *Ik ruim de keuken op.* · *Zet je de wekker uit?* · *Bel je me later terug?*

**Produce:**
1. Put *opstaan* in a sentence about tomorrow morning (correct split).
2. Fix: *Ik opsta om zes* → correct order.

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — final -n:** *eten, gaan* — don’t swallow the *-n* completely in careful speech.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**present tense (weak verbs, common irregulars: zijn, hebben, gaan)**
• NL: We hebben vanavond een afspraak bij vrienden. / Ik werk vier dagen op kantoor.
• EN: We have an appointment with friends tonight. / I work four days in the office.

**word order in main clauses and yes/no questions**
• NL: Hoe laat sta jij meestal op? / Ga je morgen naar je cursus?
• EN: What time do you usually get up? / Are you going to your class tomorrow?

**separable verbs in present (opstaan, meenemen)**
• NL: Zet je de wekker vroeg voor je rooster? / Ik ruim de keuken op na het eten.
• EN: Do you set the alarm early for your schedule? / I tidy the kitchen after dinner.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

**4 — Choose best closing:** A) Groetjes B) Met vriendelijke groet C) Doei

**5 — Word order:** fix: *Ik wil graag een afspraak morgen.*

**6 — Scenario match:** pick phrase: **Ik meld een storing.**

**7 — Read aloud** your improved message.

**8 — Check:** is the request clear in one glance?
---

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*

**Dutch culture insight**

**Agenda culture** shows up in everyday language: *Hoe laat*, *afspraak*, and *in de pauze* pop up in offices and schools. Being a few minutes late usually gets a quick *Sorry, vertraging* rather than a long excuse.

**Phrases to steal (Dutch → English)**

steps.activity:
**Culture snapshot — the Netherlands**

Many Dutch people plan social visits in advance; spontaneous dropping by is less common than in some cultures.

**Mini text (Dutch):**
In veel appartementen staat een huisregel: na 22:00 rust op de gang. Buren lossen kleine problemen vaak eerst met een kort gesprek.

*In many flats there is a rule: quiet in the corridor after 22:00. Neighbours often solve small issues with a short chat first.*
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — v / f:** *vandaag, vier* — light *v*, not English *w*.

**Useful phrases for talking about customs**

• **In Nederland …** — In the Netherlands …
• **Bij ons …** — Where I'm from …
• **Mag ik vragen …?** — May I ask …?

**Context:** Many Dutch people plan social visits in advance; spontaneous dropping by is less common than in some cultures.

**Say or write:** one respectful question you could ask a Dutch friend about a tradition.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Match:** Koningsdag — orange / Sinterklaas — December gifts / kerst — family meal (discuss).

**2 — Institution:** where do you register an address? *(hint: **gemeente**)*

**3 — Sort:** "Direct can still be polite" — **waar / niet waar**?

**4 — Gist:** what is **rust** on a building notice about?

**5 — Reflect:** one difference between your country and NL (English ok).

**6 — Gap-fill:** In Nederland fietsen veel mensen, _____ het is plat.

**7 — Scenario:** friend cancels last minute — what do many Dutch people appreciate?

**8 — Your question in Dutch:** **Mag ik vragen hoe …?** finish it.

**Culture reminder:** Many Dutch people plan social visits in advance; spontaneous dropping by is less common than in some cultures.

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

items.feedbackCorrect: Yes — *Ik leer Nederlands sinds januari.*
items.feedbackIncorrect: The present of *leren* (ik-form) is **leer**.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.
items.feedbackIncorrect: This is **true**: many yes/no questions start with the verb.

items.prompt: Say or write **one new sentence** using **punctual**.
items.exampleAnswer: Example: *Ik wil graag punctual met mijn nieuwe buren.* (adjust to your situation.)

steps.activity:
**Nice work.**

Next in this unit: **Listen & read · Conversation routines you can reuse** — we keep building **People & daily rhythm** with a new focus.

**2-minute revision:** skim the phrase bank from step 6 once more.

When you're ready, open **Quiz** for a compact recap — or **Flashcards** for the lemmas.

**Dutch culture insight**

Weekend rhythm often mixes **chores, sport, and gezelligheid**: markets, terraces, or *een stukje fietsen*. Saying *Fijne weekend!* on Friday is standard in shops and at work.

**Phrases to steal (Dutch → English)**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Ben je vanavond thuis?* | Are you home tonight? |
| *Zullen we een afspraak maken?* | Shall we set a time? |
| *Sorry, ik ben een beetje te laat.* | Sorry, I'm a little late. |

**Mini dialogue**

**A:** Hé, heb je zin om zaterdag koffie te drinken?
**B:** Ja, leuk! Hoe laat ongeveer?
**A:** Rond elf uur bij het station?
**B:** Prima, ik stuur je morgen een appje.

**Say or write:** Invite someone for coffee using *Zullen we …* or *Heb je zin om …*.

*Optional reflection:* In one English sentence, what feels similar or different where you live?

steps.activity:
Welcome! This lesson is built in small steps so you always know where you are.

**What you'll be able to do:** Understand short input about people & daily rhythm, notice useful chunks, and answer gist/detail questions

**Grammar thread today:** Main clause vs yes/no question word order — you'll get explanations, examples, and production tasks in **Language focus** and **Guided practice**.

Take your time — you can repeat any step. Try to say at least one Dutch sentence out loud today.
steps.teacher_notes: Keep under ~1 min; reassure anxious learners.

steps.activity:
Let's wake up vocabulary about **People & daily rhythm**.

Words you might use today:
• **ontbijt**
• **pendelen**
• **agenda**
• **kennismaken**
• **collega**
• **familie**
• **rooster**
• **weekend**

**Your turn (1–2 short sentences, Dutch or mixed):**
**Wat deed je gisteren?** — *What did you do yesterday?*

If you only know a few words, that's fine — start there.
steps.teacher_notes: Accept partial Dutch; encourage gestures.
steps.example_response: Gisteren werkte ik tot vijf uur. Daarna fietste ik naar huis en kookte ik een eenvoudige maaltijd. 's Avonds las ik nog even en ging ik op tijd naar bed.

illustration.alt: Diagram of a day: morning (coffee), afternoon (work or study), evening (rest)

steps.activity:
**Listen & read** this short conversation. Read twice: first for gist, then for details.

**Moeder:** Lieverd, hoe laat ben je morgen thuis voor het eten?

**Tiener:** Rond half zeven, denk ik. Ik heb training.

**Moeder:** Prima. We eten warm om zeven uur.

**Tiener:** Oké. Mag ik daarna nog even huiswerk doen?

**Moeder:** Ja, maar om tien uur rust, goed?

**Gist check:** Who are the people, and what is the situation?
**Detail check:** Note one time phrase or place you recognise.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — statement vs question order**

**Rule:** In neutral statements the verb sits **after** the subject (*Ik ga morgen*). In yes/no questions the verb often moves **before** the subject (*Ga je morgen?*).

**Patterns:**
• *Ik bel je straks.* · *Bel je me straks?* · *We eten om zeven.* · *Eten jullie om zeven?*

**Produce:**
1. Turn *We gaan naar het station* into a question (verb first).
2. Turn *Kom je vanavond?* into a calm statement (subject + verb).

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — g / ch:** practise *goed — licht — dag — nacht* with TTS; back *g* vs softer *ch*.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**present tense (weak verbs, common irregulars: zijn, hebben, gaan)**
• NL: We hebben vanavond een afspraak bij vrienden. / Ik werk vier dagen op kantoor.
• EN: We have an appointment with friends tonight. / I work four days in the office.

**word order in main clauses and yes/no questions**
• NL: Hoe laat sta jij meestal op? / Ga je morgen naar je cursus?
• EN: What time do you usually get up? / Are you going to your class tomorrow?

**separable verbs in present (opstaan, meenemen)**
• NL: Zet je de wekker vroeg voor je rooster? / Ik ruim de keuken op na het eten.
• EN: Do you set the alarm early for your schedule? / I tidy the kitchen after dinner.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Match** (say the pairs out loud)
• **ontbijt** ↔ a word you connect with daily life
• **pendelen** ↔ a place or thing from this unit

**2 — Gap-fill**
Complete: Ik _____ om acht uur. *(opstaan → stem: Ik **sta** … **op**)*

**3 — Mini dialogue**
**A:** Hoe laat ben je klaar? **B:** Om half zes, denk ik.

**4 — Find the odd one**
Which word fits *least* with this unit: **agenda**, **broodje**, **trein**? Why?

**5 — True / false**
Say **waar** or **niet waar**: "In Dutch, the verb often moves when you ask a yes/no question."

**6 — Your sentence**
Write one true sentence about yourself using **ontbijt** or **pendelen**.

**7 — Transform**
Make this informal line a bit more formal: "Kun je me helpen?" → start with **Kunt u …**

**8 — Quick translation check**
"We hebben vanavond een afspraak bij vrienden." — what does it mean in English (your own words)?
---

**All four skills — finish this lesson with these four micro-tasks**

🎧 **Listening:** Use TTS on this Dutch line (slow, then closer to natural): *Moeder: Lieverd, hoe laat ben je morgen thuis voor het eten?*

📖 **Reading:** Read silently, then say the **main idea in one English sentence**: *Moeder: Lieverd, hoe laat ben je morgen thuis voor het eten?*

✍️ **Writing:** Write **one new Dutch sentence** that uses **ontbijt** and fits *Main clause vs yes/no question word order*.

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*
steps.teacher_notes: Mix modalities; at least 6 tasks — all concrete above.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.
items.feedbackIncorrect: This is **true**: many yes/no questions start with the verb.

**Dutch culture insight**

**Punctuality** for trains and classes is valued, but social time can be looser. *Ongeveer*, *rond*, and *een beetje later* help you sound natural when plans are soft.

**Phrases to steal (Dutch → English)**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Ben je vanavond thuis?* | Are you home tonight? |
| *Zullen we een afspraak maken?* | Shall we set a time? |
| *Sorry, ik ben een beetje te laat.* | Sorry, I'm a little late. |

**Mini dialogue**

**A:** Hé, heb je zin om zaterdag koffie te drinken?
**B:** Ja, leuk! Hoe laat ongeveer?
**A:** Rond elf uur bij het station?
**B:** Prima, ik stuur je morgen een appje.

**Say or write:** Invite someone for coffee using *Zullen we …* or *Heb je zin om …*.

*Optional reflection:* In one English sentence, what feels similar or different where you live?

steps.activity:

**What you'll be able to do:** Understand short input about food & shopping, notice useful chunks, and answer gist/detail questions

**Grammar thread today:** Modals for requests: willen, kunnen, mogen — you'll get explanations, examples, and production tasks in **Language focus** and **Guided practice**.

Take your time — you can repeat any step. Try to say at least one Dutch sentence out loud today.
steps.teacher_notes: Keep under ~1 min; reassure anxious learners.

steps.activity:
Let's wake up vocabulary about **Food & shopping**.

Words you might use today:
• **boodschappen**
• **bonuskaart**
• **kassa**
• **bon**
• **pinnen**
• **contant**
• **vegetarisch**
• **notenallergie**

**Your turn (1–2 short sentences, Dutch or mixed):**
**Wat deed je gisteren?** — *What did you do yesterday?*

If you only know a few words, that's fine — start there.
steps.teacher_notes: Accept partial Dutch; encourage gestures.
steps.example_response: Gisteren ging ik naar de supermarkt en kocht ik brood en groente. 's Middags maakte ik een salade en 's avonds at ik met een vriend in een klein restaurant.

steps.activity:
**Listen & read** this short conversation. Read twice: first for gist, then for details.

**Klant:** Goedemiddag. Waar vind ik de lactosevrije yoghurt?

**Medewerker:** Gangpad 5, rechts. Er is ook plantaardige kaas bij de koeling.

**Klant:** Dank u. En heeft u deze week aanbieding op appels?

**Medewerker:** Ja, een kilo voor twee euro, bij de groente.

**Klant:** Perfect. Dan neem ik ook een mandje.

**Gist check:** Who are the people, and what is the situation?
**Detail check:** Note one time phrase or place you recognise.

---

**Script — Albert Heijn (checkout)**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| Mag ik een bon, alstublieft? | May I have a receipt, please? |
| Ik pin graag contactloos. | I'd like to pay contactless. |
| Statiegeld flesjes, alstublieft. | Bottle deposit refund, please. |

**Listen task:** play TTS on the Dutch column once, then cover the English and say what each line means.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — modals (requests & permission)**

**Rule:** *Willen* (want), *kunnen* (can), *mogen* (may) + infinitive at the end: *Ik **wil** graag **betalen***.

**Patterns:**
• *Mag ik een bon?* · *Kunt u me helpen?* · *Ik kan vandaag niet komen.*

**Produce:**
1. Ask permission with *Mag ik …?* for something you need in a shop.
2. Polite *Kunt u …* vs informal *Kun je …* — write one of each.

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — ui / eu / ij:** round lips for *huis — leeuw*; *mijn* is one smooth glide.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**modal verbs: willen, kunnen, mogen (polite requests)**
• NL: Ik wil graag halfvolle melk. / Mag ik een bon, alstublieft?
• EN: I'd like semi-skimmed milk. / May I have a receipt, please?

**measure words and containers (een kilo, een pak, een fles)**
• NL: Neemt u een mandje bij de ingang. / Kiest u pin of contant?
• EN: Take a basket at the entrance. / Choose card or cash?

**comparatives (goedkoper, lekkerder) in short opinions**
• NL: Deze kaas is iets goedkoper vandaag. / Ik vind vers brood lekkerder dan uit de diepvries.
• EN: This cheese is a bit cheaper today. / I prefer fresh bread to frozen.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.


**Culture tip:** Supermarkets like Albert Heijn and Jumbo are widespread; many people bring their own bags (statiegeld bottles returned to machines).
*How does this compare with where you live? One sentence in your own words.*
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

Next in this unit: **Listen & read · Mini-dialogues from everyday life** — we keep building **Food & shopping** with a new focus.

When you're ready, open **Quiz** for a compact recap — or **Flashcards** for the lemmas.

**Dutch culture insight**

**Albert Heijn**, **Jumbo**, **Lidl**, and **Dirk** are everyday names; many people shop several times a week for fresh bread and veg. Bringing your own bag is normal; bottle returns (*statiegeld*) at the machine are part of the routine.

**Phrases — supermarket & paying**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Mag ik een bon, alstublieft?* | May I have a receipt, please? |
| *Ik pin graag contactloos.* | I'd like to pay contactless. |
| *Waar vind ik de lactosevrije melk?* | Where do I find lactose-free milk? |

**Mini dialogue**

**Klant:** Goedemiddag, ik zoek volkoren brood en kaas zonder noten.
**Medewerker:** De kaas is bij de toonbank; het brood ligt in gangpad twee.
**Klant:** Dank u. Ik betaal bij zelfscankassa vier.

**Say or write:** Ask where one product is using *Waar vind ik …?*

steps.activity:
Welcome! This lesson is built in small steps so you always know where you are.

**What you'll be able to do:** Understand short input about food & shopping, notice useful chunks, and answer gist/detail questions

**Grammar thread today:** Polite imperatives in shops (Neemt u …, Geeft u …) — you'll get explanations, examples, and production tasks in **Language focus** and **Guided practice**.

Take your time — you can repeat any step. Try to say at least one Dutch sentence out loud today.
steps.teacher_notes: Keep under ~1 min; reassure anxious learners.

steps.activity:
Let's wake up vocabulary about **Food & shopping**.

Words you might use today:
• **bon**
• **pinnen**
• **contant**
• **vegetarisch**
• **notenallergie**
• **aanbieding**
• **vers**
• **diepvries**

**Your turn (1–2 short sentences, Dutch or mixed):**
**Wat deed je gisteren?** — *What did you do yesterday?*

If you only know a few words, that's fine — start there.
steps.teacher_notes: Accept partial Dutch; encourage gestures.
steps.example_response: Gisteren ging ik naar de supermarkt en kocht ik brood en groente. 's Middags maakte ik een salade en 's avonds at ik met een vriend in een klein restaurant.

steps.activity:
**Listen & read** this short conversation. Read twice: first for gist, then for details.

**Bakker:** Goedemorgen! Wat mag het zijn?

**Klant:** Twee bruine bolletjes en een croissant, alstublieft.

**Bakker:** Wil je ze gesneden?

**Klant:** Nee, heel is goed. Betaal ik met pin?

**Bakker:** Ja hoor. Dat wordt drie euro vijftig.

**Gist check:** Who are the people, and what is the situation?
**Detail check:** Note one time phrase or place you recognise.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — polite imperatives (service Dutch)**

**Rule:** Staff often use *u* + verb: *Neemt u …*, *Wacht u even*. You mirror their *u* / *je*.

**Patterns:**
• *Neemt u een mandje.* · *Geeft u mij een kassabon.* · *Betaalt u contactloos.*

**Produce:**
1. Write one polite request you might hear at a counter.
2. Reply with *Ja, graag* or *Nee, dank u* appropriately.

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — sch / sj:** *school* vs *sjabloon* — listen twice, then shadow.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**modal verbs: willen, kunnen, mogen (polite requests)**
• NL: Mag ik een bon, alstublieft? / Ik wil graag halfvolle melk.
• EN: May I have a receipt, please? / I'd like semi-skimmed milk.

**measure words and containers (een kilo, een pak, een fles)**
• NL: Kiest u pin of contant? / Neemt u een mandje bij de ingang.
• EN: Choose card or cash? / Take a basket at the entrance.

**comparatives (goedkoper, lekkerder) in short opinions**
• NL: Ik vind vers brood lekkerder dan uit de diepvries. / Deze kaas is iets goedkoper vandaag.
• EN: I prefer fresh bread to frozen. / This cheese is a bit cheaper today.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Match** (say the pairs out loud)
• **bon** ↔ a word you connect with daily life
• **pinnen** ↔ a place or thing from this unit

**2 — Gap-fill**
Complete: Ik _____ om acht uur. *(opstaan → stem: Ik **sta** … **op**)*

**3 — Mini dialogue**
**A:** Hoe laat ben je klaar? **B:** Om half zes, denk ik.

**4 — Find the odd one**
Which word fits *least* with this unit: **contant**, **broodje**, **trein**? Why?

**5 — True / false**
Say **waar** or **niet waar**: "In Dutch, the verb often moves when you ask a yes/no question."

**6 — Your sentence**
Write one true sentence about yourself using **bon** or **pinnen**.

**7 — Transform**
Make this informal line a bit more formal: "Kun je me helpen?" → start with **Kunt u …**

**8 — Quick translation check**
"Mag ik een bon, alstublieft?" — what does it mean in English (your own words)?
---

**All four skills — finish this lesson with these four micro-tasks**

🎧 **Listening:** Use TTS on this Dutch line (slow, then closer to natural): *Bakker: Goedemorgen! Wat mag het zijn?*

📖 **Reading:** Read silently, then say the **main idea in one English sentence**: *Bakker: Goedemorgen! Wat mag het zijn?*

✍️ **Writing:** Write **one new Dutch sentence** that uses **bon** and fits *Polite imperatives in shops (Neemt u …, Geeft u …)*.

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*
steps.teacher_notes: Mix modalities; at least 6 tasks — all concrete above.

steps.activity:
**Phrase bank (use freely)**
• bon
• pinnen
• contant
• vegetarisch
• notenallergie
• aanbieding
• vers
• diepvries
• mandje
• boodschappen
• bonuskaart
• kassa

**Your task:** Write or say **4–6 short sentences** in Dutch about this unit theme.
Rules: include **at least one question** and one connector (**en**, **maar**, or **want**).

**Example starter (you can change it):**
Vandaag … Ik wil … **want** … **En jij?**
steps.teacher_notes: Encourage intelligibility over perfection.

steps.activity:
**Quick self-check**

Try each question, then tap **Check answer**. The correct answer stays hidden until you submit.

**Tip:** Say Dutch options out loud if that helps.

**Afterwards:** If three or more felt easy, open **Quiz** for a short recap. If not, go back to **Guided practice** once more.
steps.teacher_notes: Rendered with interactive UI when interaction is present.

items.prompt: Pick the best reply when someone says **Hoe gaat het?**
items.feedbackCorrect: Nice — this is a natural, friendly reply.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Tot ziens** is goodbye; **Graag gedaan** fits after *dank je*. After *Hoe gaat het?* people often say how they feel, e.g. **Goed, dank je!**

items.prompt: Complete: **Ik ___ Nederlands sinds januari.**
items.hint: Present tense of *leren* (to learn), 1st person singular.
items.feedbackCorrect: Yes — *Ik leer Nederlands sinds januari.*
items.feedbackIncorrect: The present of *leren* (ik-form) is **leer**.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.
items.feedbackIncorrect: This is **true**: many yes/no questions start with the verb.

items.prompt: Say or write **one new sentence** using **bon**.
items.exampleAnswer: Example: *Ik wil graag bon met mijn nieuwe buren.* (adjust to your situation.)

steps.activity:
**Nice work.**

Next in this unit: **Patterns & drills · Drills that prep you for freer speech** — we keep building **Food & shopping** with a new focus.

**2-minute revision:** skim the phrase bank from step 6 once more.

When you're ready, open **Quiz** for a compact recap — or **Flashcards** for the lemmas.

**Dutch culture insight**

At the bakery or cheese counter, **Dank u** / **Alstublieft** and clear eye contact go a long way. Staff may switch to *je* if you sound relaxed; mirroring their *u* / *je* is a safe habit.

**Phrases — supermarket & paying**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Mag ik een bon, alstublieft?* | May I have a receipt, please? |
| *Ik pin graag contactloos.* | I'd like to pay contactless. |
| *Waar vind ik de lactosevrije melk?* | Where do I find lactose-free milk? |

**Mini dialogue**

**Klant:** Goedemiddag, ik zoek volkoren brood en kaas zonder noten.
**Medewerker:** De kaas is bij de toonbank; het brood ligt in gangpad twee.
**Klant:** Dank u. Ik betaal bij zelfscankassa vier.

**Say or write:** Ask where one product is using *Waar vind ik …?*

*Optional reflection:* In one English sentence, what feels similar or different where you live?
steps.teacher_notes: Link to next lesson id in CMS when wired.

quiz_ideas: Self-check: choose the best reply to "Hoe gaat het?" (see step 7 in the lesson).
Gap-fill idea: write a sentence with **bon** in the present tense.
True/false: in Dutch yes/no questions the verb often comes before the subject.
Say one new polite request starting with **Kunt u …** or **Mag ik …**.
Paraphrase one Dutch line from the lesson input in your own English.
assessment.success_criteria: You can complete the step-7 self-check comfortably and produce at least one clear Dutch sentence about the lesson theme (spoken or written).

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — comparatives & short opinions**

**Rule:** *-er* often marks comparison (*goedkoper*, *beter*). *Dan* compares: *lekkerder **dan***.

**Patterns:**
• *Dit brood is verser.* · *Ik vind thee lekkerder dan koffie.*

**Produce:**
1. Compare two products in one Dutch sentence (*…er dan …*).
2. Say what you prefer this week using *Ik vind …*.

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — stress:** stress content words: *Ik wil een afspraak maken* (not every syllable equal).

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**modal verbs: willen, kunnen, mogen (polite requests)**
• NL: Ik wil graag halfvolle melk. / Mag ik een bon, alstublieft?
• EN: I'd like semi-skimmed milk. / May I have a receipt, please?

**measure words and containers (een kilo, een pak, een fles)**
• NL: Neemt u een mandje bij de ingang. / Kiest u pin of contant?
• EN: Take a basket at the entrance. / Choose card or cash?

**comparatives (goedkoper, lekkerder) in short opinions**
• NL: Deze kaas is iets goedkoper vandaag. / Ik vind vers brood lekkerder dan uit de diepvries.
• EN: This cheese is a bit cheaper today. / I prefer fresh bread to frozen.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**Reading focus**

Read this real-life style text slowly.

[Supermarktfolder]
Weekaanbieding: 2 pakken melk voor €2,20. Bonus: appeltaart vers gebakken in de winkel.
Let op: zonder notenvariant beschikbaar bij de bakkerijbalie.
Openingstijden za: 08:00–21:00, zo: 10:00–20:00.

**After reading:** in one English sentence, what is this text mainly about?
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — modals (requests & permission)**

**Rule:** *Willen* (want), *kunnen* (can), *mogen* (may) + infinitive at the end: *Ik **wil** graag **betalen***.

**Patterns:**
• *Mag ik een bon?* · *Kunt u me helpen?* · *Ik kan vandaag niet komen.*

**Produce:**
1. Ask permission with *Mag ik …?* for something you need in a shop.
2. Polite *Kunt u …* vs informal *Kun je …* — write one of each.

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — rhythm:** short chunks: *van de — naar het — met een* — run them together softly.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**modal verbs: willen, kunnen, mogen (polite requests)**
• NL: Mag ik een bon, alstublieft? / Ik wil graag halfvolle melk.
• EN: May I have a receipt, please? / I'd like semi-skimmed milk.

**measure words and containers (een kilo, een pak, een fles)**
• NL: Kiest u pin of contant? / Neemt u een mandje bij de ingang.
• EN: Choose card or cash? / Take a basket at the entrance.

**comparatives (goedkoper, lekkerder) in short opinions**
• NL: Ik vind vers brood lekkerder dan uit de diepvries. / Deze kaas is iets goedkoper vandaag.
• EN: I prefer fresh bread to frozen. / This cheese is a bit cheaper today.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Headline:** give this thread a title in English (max 6 words).

**2 — Times:** list every time or date you see.

**3 — Who must do what?** One sentence in English.

**4 — Gap-fill from text:** "De lift is morgen … voor onderhoud." *(from context)*

**5 — Problem / solution:** what is the problem and what is suggested?

**6 — Word guess:** what might **onderhoud** mean?

**7 — True / false:** "Everyone is angry in this chat."

**8 — Your reply:** write one short Dutch sentence you could post in this group.

**Text:**
[Supermarktfolder]
Weekaanbieding: 2 pakken melk voor €2,20. Bonus: appeltaart vers gebakken in de winkel.
Let op: zonder notenvariant beschikbaar bij de bakkerijbalie.
Openingstijden za: 08:00–21:00, zo: 10:00–20:00.
---

📖 **Reading:** Read silently, then say the **main idea in one English sentence**: *[Supermarktfolder]*

✍️ **Writing:** Write **one new Dutch sentence** that uses **vers** and fits *Modals for requests: willen, kunnen, mogen*.

**Dutch culture insight**

Markets and **weekly offers** (*aanbieding*, *1+1 gratis*) are small-talk fuel. Commenting on the weather while queuing is classic Dutch small talk.

**Klant:** Dank u. Ik betaal bij zelfscankassa vier.

steps.activity:
**Speaking setup (you can stay text-only)**

You are practising short exchanges. Read both roles aloud, then swap.

Rol A/B dialogues (text-first). Gebruik frames: A: Wat vind je van …? B: Ik vind … want … | A: Zullen we …? B: Goed idee / Liever niet, want …

Tip: add **alstublieft** / **dank u wel** in formal situations.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — polite imperatives (service Dutch)**

**Rule:** Staff often use *u* + verb: *Neemt u …*, *Wacht u even*. You mirror their *u* / *je*.

**Patterns:**
• *Neemt u een mandje.* · *Geeft u mij een kassabon.* · *Betaalt u contactloos.*

**Produce:**
1. Write one polite request you might hear at a counter.
2. Reply with *Ja, graag* or *Nee, dank u* appropriately.

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — final -n:** *eten, gaan* — don’t swallow the *-n* completely in careful speech.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**modal verbs: willen, kunnen, mogen (polite requests)**
• NL: Ik wil graag halfvolle melk. / Mag ik een bon, alstublieft?
• EN: I'd like semi-skimmed milk. / May I have a receipt, please?

**measure words and containers (een kilo, een pak, een fles)**
• NL: Neemt u een mandje bij de ingang. / Kiest u pin of contant?
• EN: Take a basket at the entrance. / Choose card or cash?

**comparatives (goedkoper, lekkerder) in short opinions**
• NL: Deze kaas is iets goedkoper vandaag. / Ik vind vers brood lekkerder dan uit de diepvries.
• EN: This cheese is a bit cheaper today. / I prefer fresh bread to frozen.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**Mini situation**

You get a short message that sounds a bit too direct in Dutch. Your job in the next steps is to choose a polite fix.

**Message (Dutch):**
Jullie kaas was beschimmeld. Ik wil nu geld terug. Spoed.

**Your cheese was mouldy. I want money back now. Urgent.**

We will upgrade the tone without changing the facts.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — comparatives & short opinions**

**Rule:** *-er* often marks comparison (*goedkoper*, *beter*). *Dan* compares: *lekkerder **dan***.

**Patterns:**
• *Dit brood is verser.* · *Ik vind thee lekkerder dan koffie.*

**Produce:**
1. Compare two products in one Dutch sentence (*…er dan …*).
2. Say what you prefer this week using *Ik vind …*.

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — v / f:** *vandaag, vier* — light *v*, not English *w*.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**modal verbs: willen, kunnen, mogen (polite requests)**
• NL: Mag ik een bon, alstublieft? / Ik wil graag halfvolle melk.
• EN: May I have a receipt, please? / I'd like semi-skimmed milk.

**measure words and containers (een kilo, een pak, een fles)**
• NL: Kiest u pin of contant? / Neemt u een mandje bij de ingang.
• EN: Choose card or cash? / Take a basket at the entrance.

**comparatives (goedkoper, lekkerder) in short opinions**
• NL: Ik vind vers brood lekkerder dan uit de diepvries. / Deze kaas is iets goedkoper vandaag.
• EN: I prefer fresh bread to frozen. / This cheese is a bit cheaper today.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.
