**Grammar thread today:** Modals for requests: willen, kunnen, mogen — you'll get explanations, examples, and production tasks in **Language focus** and **Guided practice**.

Take your time — you can repeat any step. Try to say at least one Dutch sentence out loud today.
steps.teacher_notes: Keep under ~1 min; reassure anxious learners.

steps.activity:
Let's wake up vocabulary about **Food & shopping**.

Words you might use today:
• **vegetarisch**
• **notenallergie**
• **aanbieding**
• **vers**
• **diepvries**
• **mandje**
• **boodschappen**
• **bonuskaart**

**Your turn (1–2 short sentences, Dutch or mixed):**
**Wat deed je gisteren?** — *What did you do yesterday?*

If you only know a few words, that's fine — start there.
steps.teacher_notes: Accept partial Dutch; encourage gestures.
steps.example_response: Gisteren ging ik naar de supermarkt en kocht ik brood en groente. 's Middags maakte ik een salade en 's avonds at ik met een vriend in een klein restaurant.

steps.activity:
**Culture snapshot — the Netherlands**

Supermarkets like Albert Heijn and Jumbo are widespread; many people bring their own bags (statiegeld bottles returned to machines).

**Mini text (Dutch):**
In veel appartementen staat een huisregel: na 22:00 rust op de gang. Buren lossen kleine problemen vaak eerst met een kort gesprek.

*In many flats there is a rule: quiet in the corridor after 22:00. Neighbours often solve small issues with a short chat first.*
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — g / ch:** practise *goed — licht — dag — nacht* with TTS; back *g* vs softer *ch*.

**Useful phrases for talking about customs**

• **In Nederland …** — In the Netherlands …
• **Bij ons …** — Where I'm from …
• **Mag ik vragen …?** — May I ask …?

**Context:** Supermarkets like Albert Heijn and Jumbo are widespread; many people bring their own bags (statiegeld bottles returned to machines).

**Say or write:** one respectful question you could ask a Dutch friend about a tradition.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Match:** Koningsdag — orange / Sinterklaas — December gifts / kerst — family meal (discuss).

**2 — Institution:** where do you register an address? *(hint: **gemeente**)*

**3 — Sort:** "Direct can still be polite" — **waar / niet waar**?

**4 — Gist:** what is **rust** on a building notice about?

**5 — Reflect:** one difference between your country and NL (English ok).

**6 — Gap-fill:** In Nederland fietsen veel mensen, _____ het is plat.

**7 — Scenario:** friend cancels last minute — what do many Dutch people appreciate?

**8 — Your question in Dutch:** **Mag ik vragen hoe …?** finish it.

**Culture reminder:** Supermarkets like Albert Heijn and Jumbo are widespread; many people bring their own bags (statiegeld bottles returned to machines).
---

**All four skills — finish this lesson with these four micro-tasks**

🎧 **Listening:** Use TTS on this Dutch line (slow, then closer to natural): *Ik wil graag halfvolle melk.*

📖 **Reading:** Read silently, then say the **main idea in one English sentence**: *Ik wil graag halfvolle melk.*

✍️ **Writing:** Write **one new Dutch sentence** that uses **vegetarisch** and fits *Modals for requests: willen, kunnen, mogen*.

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*
steps.teacher_notes: Mix modalities; at least 6 tasks — all concrete above.

steps.activity:
**Phrase bank (use freely)**
• vegetarisch
• notenallergie
• aanbieding
• vers
• diepvries
• mandje
• boodschappen
• bonuskaart
• kassa
• bon

**Your task:** Write or say **4–6 short sentences** in Dutch about this unit theme.
Rules: include **at least one question** and one connector (**en**, **maar**, or **want**).

**Example starter (you can change it):**
Vandaag … Ik wil … **want** … **En jij?**
steps.teacher_notes: Encourage intelligibility over perfection.

steps.activity:
**Quick self-check**

Try each question, then tap **Check answer**. The correct answer stays hidden until you submit.

**Tip:** Say Dutch options out loud if that helps.

**Afterwards:** If three or more felt easy, open **Quiz** for a short recap. If not, go back to **Guided practice** once more.
steps.teacher_notes: Rendered with interactive UI when interaction is present.

items.prompt: Pick the best reply when someone says **Hoe gaat het?**
items.feedbackCorrect: Nice — this is a natural, friendly reply.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Tot ziens** is goodbye; **Graag gedaan** fits after *dank je*. After *Hoe gaat het?* people often say how they feel, e.g. **Goed, dank je!**

items.prompt: Complete: **Ik ___ Nederlands sinds januari.**
items.hint: Present tense of *leren* (to learn), 1st person singular.
items.feedbackCorrect: Yes — *Ik leer Nederlands sinds januari.*
items.feedbackIncorrect: The present of *leren* (ik-form) is **leer**.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.
items.feedbackIncorrect: This is **true**: many yes/no questions start with the verb.

items.prompt: Say or write **one new sentence** using **vegetarisch**.
items.exampleAnswer: Example: *Ik wil graag vegetarisch met mijn nieuwe buren.* (adjust to your situation.)

steps.activity:
**Nice work.**

Next in this unit: **Real-life task · Fix a polite message—real stakes** — we keep building **Food & shopping** with a new focus.

**2-minute revision:** skim the phrase bank from step 6 once more.

When you're ready, open **Quiz** for a compact recap — or **Flashcards** for the lemmas.

**Dutch culture insight**

**Tikkie** (payment requests between friends) is everywhere after shared dinners or trips. Saying *Ik stuur je een Tikkie* is ordinary, not stingy.

**Phrases — supermarket & paying**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Mag ik een bon, alstublieft?* | May I have a receipt, please? |
| *Ik pin graag contactloos.* | I'd like to pay contactless. |
| *Waar vind ik de lactosevrije melk?* | Where do I find lactose-free milk? |

**Mini dialogue**

**Klant:** Goedemiddag, ik zoek volkoren brood en kaas zonder noten.
**Medewerker:** De kaas is bij de toonbank; het brood ligt in gangpad twee.
**Klant:** Dank u. Ik betaal bij zelfscankassa vier.

**Say or write:** Ask where one product is using *Waar vind ik …?*

*Optional reflection:* In one English sentence, what feels similar or different where you live?
steps.teacher_notes: Link to next lesson id in CMS when wired.

quiz_ideas: Self-check: choose the best reply to "Hoe gaat het?" (see step 7 in the lesson).
Gap-fill idea: write a sentence with **vegetarisch** in the present tense.
True/false: in Dutch yes/no questions the verb often comes before the subject.
Say one new polite request starting with **Kunt u …** or **Mag ik …**.
Paraphrase one Dutch line from the lesson input in your own English.
assessment.success_criteria: You can complete the step-7 self-check comfortably and produce at least one clear Dutch sentence about the lesson theme (spoken or written).

steps.activity:
**Mini situation**

You get a short message that sounds a bit too direct in Dutch. Your job in the next steps is to choose a polite fix.

**Message (Dutch):**
Jullie kaas was beschimmeld. Ik wil nu geld terug. Spoed.

**Your cheese was mouldy. I want money back now. Urgent.**

We will upgrade the tone without changing the facts.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — comparatives & short opinions**

**Rule:** *-er* often marks comparison (*goedkoper*, *beter*). *Dan* compares: *lekkerder **dan***.

**Patterns:**
• *Dit brood is verser.* · *Ik vind thee lekkerder dan koffie.*

**Produce:**
1. Compare two products in one Dutch sentence (*…er dan …*).
2. Say what you prefer this week using *Ik vind …*.

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — ui / eu / ij:** round lips for *huis — leeuw*; *mijn* is one smooth glide.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**modal verbs: willen, kunnen, mogen (polite requests)**
• NL: Mag ik een bon, alstublieft? / Ik wil graag halfvolle melk.
• EN: May I have a receipt, please? / I'd like semi-skimmed milk.

**measure words and containers (een kilo, een pak, een fles)**
• NL: Kiest u pin of contant? / Neemt u een mandje bij de ingang.
• EN: Choose card or cash? / Take a basket at the entrance.

**comparatives (goedkoper, lekkerder) in short opinions**
• NL: Ik vind vers brood lekkerder dan uit de diepvries. / Deze kaas is iets goedkoper vandaag.
• EN: I prefer fresh bread to frozen. / This cheese is a bit cheaper today.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Tone:** which line feels too blunt for a landlord?

**2 — Rewrite opening** with **Goedemiddag** + **met vriendelijke groet** at the end.

**3 — Add one sentence** with the reason: *want de verwarming uitvalt bij kou.*

**4 — Choose best closing:** A) Groetjes B) Met vriendelijke groet C) Doei

**5 — Word order:** fix: *Ik wil graag een afspraak morgen.*

**6 — Scenario match:** pick phrase: **Ik meld een storing.**

**7 — Read aloud** your improved message.

**8 — Check:** is the request clear in one glance?

grammar_points.point: prepositions of place (naast, boven, achter, tussen)
examples_nl: Er is een kleine lek in de badkamer.
Er zijn twee fietsenstallingen bij het flatgebouw.
examples_en: There is a small leak in the bathroom.

examples_nl: Onze huur is inclusief servicekosten.

examples_en: Our rent includes service charges.
Your key is on the kitchen table.

examples_nl: De buren wonen boven ons appartement.

examples_en: The neighbours live above our flat.
I'm calling the landlord about the ceiling.

steps.activity:
Welcome! This lesson is built in small steps so you always know where you are.

**What you'll be able to do:** Understand short input about housing & neighbourhood, notice useful chunks, and answer gist/detail questions

**Grammar thread today:** Er is / er zijn + place — you'll get explanations, examples, and production tasks in **Language focus** and **Guided practice**.

Take your time — you can repeat any step. Try to say at least one Dutch sentence out loud today.
steps.teacher_notes: Keep under ~1 min; reassure anxious learners.

steps.activity:
Let's wake up vocabulary about **Housing & neighbourhood**.

Words you might use today:
• **huur**
• **borg**
• **sleutel**
• **buren**
• **lift**
• **kelder**
• **dak**
• **lek**

**Your turn (1–2 short sentences, Dutch or mixed):**
**Wat deed je gisteren?** — *What did you do yesterday?*

If you only know a few words, that's fine — start there.
steps.teacher_notes: Accept partial Dutch; encourage gestures.
steps.example_response: Gisteren belde ik de verhuurder over een klein probleem. Daarna deed ik boodschappen in de buurt en ruimde ik de keuken op.

steps.activity:
**Listen & read** this short conversation. Read twice: first for gist, then for details.

**Buur:** Hoi, ik ben nieuw hier. Mag ik de containers voor plastic vragen?

**Buurvrouw:** Zeker. Gele bak buiten, links van de ingang. Papier is blauw.

**Buur:** Bedankt! En mag ik 's avonds stofzuigen?

**Buurvrouw:** Tot 22:00 is het meestal oké. Daarna liever rust.

**Gist check:** Who are the people, and what is the situation?
**Detail check:** Note one time phrase or place you recognise.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — *er* + place**

**Rule:** *Er is / er zijn* introduces existence; prepositions pin location (*in, op, naast*).

**Patterns:**
• *Er is een lift.* · *Er zijn twee fietsenstallingen.* · *De sleutel ligt op tafel.*

**Produce:**
1. Describe your home with *Er is …* / *Er zijn …*.
2. Add one place preposition (*naast de deur*).

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — sch / sj:** *school* vs *sjabloon* — listen twice, then shadow.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**prepositions of place (naast, boven, achter, tussen)**
• NL: Er is een kleine lek in de badkamer. / Er zijn twee fietsenstallingen bij het flatgebouw.
• EN: There is a small leak in the bathroom. / There are two bike sheds by the block.

**er + locative (er is, er zijn)**
• NL: Onze huur is inclusief servicekosten. / Jouw sleutel ligt op de keukentafel.
• EN: Our rent includes service charges. / Your key is on the kitchen table.

**possessive pronouns (mijn, jouw, zijn/haar, ons)**
• NL: De buren wonen boven ons appartement. / Ik bel de verhuurder over het plafond.
• EN: The neighbours live above our flat. / I'm calling the landlord about the ceiling.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.


**Culture tip:** Flats often share a central staircase (portiek); quiet hours (rust) after 22:00 are common in building rules.
*How does this compare with where you live? One sentence in your own words.*
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Match** (say the pairs out loud)
• **huur** ↔ a word you connect with daily life
• **borg** ↔ a place or thing from this unit

**2 — Gap-fill**
Complete: Ik _____ om acht uur. *(opstaan → stem: Ik **sta** … **op**)*

**3 — Mini dialogue**
**A:** Hoe laat ben je klaar? **B:** Om half zes, denk ik.

**4 — Find the odd one**
Which word fits *least* with this unit: **sleutel**, **broodje**, **trein**? Why?

**5 — True / false**
Say **waar** or **niet waar**: "In Dutch, the verb often moves when you ask a yes/no question."

**6 — Your sentence**
Write one true sentence about yourself using **huur** or **borg**.

**7 — Transform**
Make this informal line a bit more formal: "Kun je me helpen?" → start with **Kunt u …**

**8 — Quick translation check**
"Er is een kleine lek in de badkamer." — what does it mean in English (your own words)?
---

**All four skills — finish this lesson with these four micro-tasks**

🎧 **Listening:** Use TTS on this Dutch line (slow, then closer to natural): *Buur: Hoi, ik ben nieuw hier. Mag ik de containers voor plastic vragen?*

📖 **Reading:** Read silently, then say the **main idea in one English sentence**: *Buur: Hoi, ik ben nieuw hier. Mag ik de containers voor plastic vragen?*

✍️ **Writing:** Write **one new Dutch sentence** that uses **huur** and fits *Er is / er zijn + place*.

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*

**2-minute revision:** skim the phrase bank from step 6 once more.

When you're ready, open **Quiz** for a compact recap — or **Flashcards** for the lemmas.

**Dutch culture insight**

**Huisregels** and **rust na 22:00** appear in many flats; neighbours often solve small issues with a short chat before involving the landlord. A calm *Goedemorgen* in the stairwell goes a long way.

**Phrases — landlord & neighbours**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Ik wil een storing melden.* | I want to report a fault. |
| *Kunt u kijken naar het plafond?* | Could you look at the ceiling? |
| *Tot welke tijd is het stil in het portiek?* | Until what time is it quiet in the stairwell? |

**Mini dialogue**

**Buur:** Goedemorgen, ik hoorde water in de badkamer.
**Jij:** O nee, ik bel de verhuurder en stuur u een foto.
**Buur:** Dank je, ik ben tot vijf uur thuis.

**Say or write:** One polite line to report a small problem at home.

*Optional reflection:* In one English sentence, what feels similar or different where you live?

steps.activity:
Welcome! This lesson is built in small steps so you always know where you are.

**What you'll be able to do:** Understand short input about housing & neighbourhood, notice useful chunks, and answer gist/detail questions

**Grammar thread today:** Possessives (mijn, jouw, onze) — you'll get explanations, examples, and production tasks in **Language focus** and **Guided practice**.

Take your time — you can repeat any step. Try to say at least one Dutch sentence out loud today.
steps.teacher_notes: Keep under ~1 min; reassure anxious learners.

steps.activity:
Let's wake up vocabulary about **Housing & neighbourhood**.

Words you might use today:
• **buren**
• **lift**
• **kelder**
• **dak**
• **lek**
• **klusjesman**
• **huisregels**
• **afvalscheiding**

**Your turn (1–2 short sentences, Dutch or mixed):**
**Wat deed je gisteren?** — *What did you do yesterday?*

If you only know a few words, that's fine — start there.
steps.teacher_notes: Accept partial Dutch; encourage gestures.
steps.example_response: Gisteren belde ik de verhuurder over een klein probleem. Daarna deed ik boodschappen in de buurt en ruimde ik de keuken op.

steps.activity:
**Listen & read** this short conversation. Read twice: first for gist, then for details.

**Huurder:** Er zit een kleine lek bij het raam. Kunnen jullie iemand sturen?

**Verhuurder:** Stuur een foto via het portaal. We plannen een monteur deze week.

**Huurder:** Oké. Moet ik thuis zijn?

**Verhuurder:** Nee, met een sleutelkluis kan het ook. We mailen een tijdslot.

**Gist check:** Who are the people, and what is the situation?
**Detail check:** Note one time phrase or place you recognise.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — possessives**

**Rule:** *mijn, jouw, zijn, haar, ons/onze, jullie* agree in a natural way with the following noun (*onze huur*).

**Patterns:**
• *Onze buren zijn rustig.* · *Jouw paspoort ligt in de la.*

**Produce:**
1. Two true sentences: *mijn …* and *onze / jullie …*.
2. Ask whose object it is: *Wiens … is dit?* (or *Van wie is …?*).

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — stress:** stress content words: *Ik wil een afspraak maken* (not every syllable equal).

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**prepositions of place (naast, boven, achter, tussen)**
• NL: Er zijn twee fietsenstallingen bij het flatgebouw. / Er is een kleine lek in de badkamer.
• EN: There are two bike sheds by the block. / There is a small leak in the bathroom.

**er + locative (er is, er zijn)**
• NL: Jouw sleutel ligt op de keukentafel. / Onze huur is inclusief servicekosten.
• EN: Your key is on the kitchen table. / Our rent includes service charges.

**possessive pronouns (mijn, jouw, zijn/haar, ons)**
• NL: Ik bel de verhuurder over het plafond. / De buren wonen boven ons appartement.
• EN: I'm calling the landlord about the ceiling. / The neighbours live above our flat.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Match** (say the pairs out loud)
• **buren** ↔ a word you connect with daily life
• **lift** ↔ a place or thing from this unit

**2 — Gap-fill**
Complete: Ik _____ om acht uur. *(opstaan → stem: Ik **sta** … **op**)*

**3 — Mini dialogue**
**A:** Hoe laat ben je klaar? **B:** Om half zes, denk ik.

**4 — Find the odd one**
Which word fits *least* with this unit: **kelder**, **broodje**, **trein**? Why?

**5 — True / false**
Say **waar** or **niet waar**: "In Dutch, the verb often moves when you ask a yes/no question."

**6 — Your sentence**
Write one true sentence about yourself using **buren** or **lift**.

**7 — Transform**
Make this informal line a bit more formal: "Kun je me helpen?" → start with **Kunt u …**

**8 — Quick translation check**
"Er zijn twee fietsenstallingen bij het flatgebouw." — what does it mean in English (your own words)?
---

**All four skills — finish this lesson with these four micro-tasks**

🎧 **Listening:** Use TTS on this Dutch line (slow, then closer to natural): *Huurder: Er zit een kleine lek bij het raam. Kunnen jullie iemand sturen?*

📖 **Reading:** Read silently, then say the **main idea in one English sentence**: *Huurder: Er zit een kleine lek bij het raam. Kunnen jullie iemand sturen?*

✍️ **Writing:** Write **one new Dutch sentence** that uses **buren** and fits *Possessives (mijn, jouw, onze)*.

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*
steps.teacher_notes: Mix modalities; at least 6 tasks — all concrete above.

items.prompt: Pick the best reply when someone says **Hoe gaat het?**
items.feedbackCorrect: Nice — this is a natural, friendly reply.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Tot ziens** is goodbye; **Graag gedaan** fits after *dank je*. After *Hoe gaat het?* people often say how they feel, e.g. **Goed, dank je!**

items.prompt: Complete: **Ik ___ Nederlands sinds januari.**
items.hint: Present tense of *leren* (to learn), 1st person singular.
items.feedbackCorrect: Yes — *Ik leer Nederlands sinds januari.*
items.feedbackIncorrect: The present of *leren* (ik-form) is **leer**.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.
items.feedbackIncorrect: This is **true**: many yes/no questions start with the verb.

items.prompt: Say or write **one new sentence** using **buren**.
items.exampleAnswer: Example: *Ik wil graag buren met mijn nieuwe buren.* (adjust to your situation.)

**Waste sorting** colours differ by gemeente; wrong bags can mean the truck leaves them behind. When in doubt, ask *Welke kleur zak voor plastic hier?*

**Phrases — landlord & neighbours**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|

| *Kunt u kijken naar het plafond?* | Could you look at the ceiling? |
| *Tot welke tijd is het stil in het portiek?* | Until what time is it quiet in the stairwell? |

**Mini dialogue**

**Buur:** Goedemorgen, ik hoorde water in de badkamer.
**Jij:** O nee, ik bel de verhuurder en stuur u een foto.
**Buur:** Dank je, ik ben tot vijf uur thuis.

**Say or write:** One polite line to report a small problem at home.

*Optional reflection:* In one English sentence, what feels similar or different where you live?

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — *er* + place**

**Rule:** *Er is / er zijn* introduces existence; prepositions pin location (*in, op, naast*).

**Patterns:**
• *Er is een lift.* · *Er zijn twee fietsenstallingen.* · *De sleutel ligt op tafel.*

**Produce:**
1. Describe your home with *Er is …* / *Er zijn …*.
2. Add one place preposition (*naast de deur*).

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — rhythm:** short chunks: *van de — naar het — met een* — run them together softly.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**prepositions of place (naast, boven, achter, tussen)**
• NL: Er is een kleine lek in de badkamer. / Er zijn twee fietsenstallingen bij het flatgebouw.

**er + locative (er is, er zijn)**
• NL: Onze huur is inclusief servicekosten. / Jouw sleutel ligt op de keukentafel.
• EN: Our rent includes service charges. / Your key is on the kitchen table.

**possessive pronouns (mijn, jouw, zijn/haar, ons)**
• NL: De buren wonen boven ons appartement. / Ik bel de verhuurder over het plafond.
• EN: The neighbours live above our flat. / I'm calling the landlord about the ceiling.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.

**Transcript** (read along on the second listen):

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — possessives**

**Rule:** *mijn, jouw, zijn, haar, ons/onze, jullie* agree in a natural way with the following noun (*onze huur*).

**Patterns:**
• *Onze buren zijn rustig.* · *Jouw paspoort ligt in de la.*

**Produce:**
1. Two true sentences: *mijn …* and *onze / jullie …*.
2. Ask whose object it is: *Wiens … is dit?* (or *Van wie is …?*).

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — final -n:** *eten, gaan* — don’t swallow the *-n* completely in careful speech.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**prepositions of place (naast, boven, achter, tussen)**
• NL: Er zijn twee fietsenstallingen bij het flatgebouw. / Er is een kleine lek in de badkamer.
• EN: There are two bike sheds by the block. / There is a small leak in the bathroom.

**er + locative (er is, er zijn)**
• NL: Jouw sleutel ligt op de keukentafel. / Onze huur is inclusief servicekosten.
• EN: Your key is on the kitchen table. / Our rent includes service charges.

**possessive pronouns (mijn, jouw, zijn/haar, ons)**
• NL: Ik bel de verhuurder over het plafond. / De buren wonen boven ons appartement.
• EN: I'm calling the landlord about the ceiling. / The neighbours live above our flat.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Before listening:** guess three words you might hear (write them).

**2 — Gist:** is this about work, family, travel, or shopping?

**3 — Numbers:** write every number or time you hear in the script.

**4 — Who:** who takes which action (choose: A / B / both)?

**5 — Polite or informal:** find one **u**-sentence and one **je/jij**-sentence.

**6 — Paraphrase:** in English, one sentence — what do they want?

**7 — Shadowing:** read one speaker's lines aloud with the transcript.

**8 — New chunk:** pick one phrase to use in your own week.

**Script reminder:**
Buurman: Hallo, ik woon boven u. Ik hoorde water in de badkamer.
Buurvrouw: O nee! Ik bel direct de verhuurder. Hebt u een foto van het plafond?
Buurman: Ja, ik stuur ze via de app. Misschien is de afvoer verstopt.
Buurvrouw: Bedankt voor het seintje. Ik ben vandaag thuis tot vijf uur.
---

**All four skills — finish this lesson with these four micro-tasks**

🎧 **Listening:** Use TTS on this Dutch line (slow, then closer to natural): *Buurman: Hallo, ik woon boven u. Ik hoorde water in de badkamer.*

📖 **Reading:** Read silently, then say the **main idea in one English sentence**: *Buurman: Hallo, ik woon boven u. Ik hoorde water in de badkamer.*

✍️ **Writing:** Write **one new Dutch sentence** that uses **huisregels** and fits *Possessives (mijn, jouw, onze)*.

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*
steps.teacher_notes: Mix modalities; at least 6 tasks — all concrete above.

items.prompt: Pick the best reply when someone says **Hoe gaat het?**
items.feedbackCorrect: Nice — this is a natural, friendly reply.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Tot ziens** is goodbye; **Graag gedaan** fits after *dank je*. After *Hoe gaat het?* people often say how they feel, e.g. **Goed, dank je!**

items.prompt: Complete: **Ik ___ Nederlands sinds januari.**
items.hint: Present tense of *leren* (to learn), 1st person singular.
items.feedbackCorrect: Yes — *Ik leer Nederlands sinds januari.*
items.feedbackIncorrect: The present of *leren* (ik-form) is **leer**.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.
items.feedbackIncorrect: This is **true**: many yes/no questions start with the verb.

items.prompt: Say or write **one new sentence** using **huisregels**.
items.exampleAnswer: Example: *Ik wil graag huisregels met mijn nieuwe buren.* (adjust to your situation.)

steps.activity:
**Nice work.**

Next in this unit: **Writing · Requests that do not sound rude** — we keep building **Housing & neighbourhood** with a new focus.

When you're ready, open **Quiz** for a compact recap — or **Flashcards** for the lemmas.

**Dutch culture insight**

**Balkon rules** and shared laundry slots sound boring until you need them. The word *buren* covers everyone from best friends to people you only nod to.

**Phrases — landlord & neighbours**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Ik wil een storing melden.* | I want to report a fault. |
| *Kunt u kijken naar het plafond?* | Could you look at the ceiling? |
| *Tot welke tijd is het stil in het portiek?* | Until what time is it quiet in the stairwell? |

**Mini dialogue**

**Buur:** Goedemorgen, ik hoorde water in de badkamer.
**Jij:** O nee, ik bel de verhuurder en stuur u een foto.
**Buur:** Dank je, ik ben tot vijf uur thuis.

**Say or write:** One polite line to report a small problem at home.

steps.activity:
**Model message (Dutch)**

Subject: Vraag over bon

Goedemiddag,

Gisteren heb ik boodschappen gedaan bij uw winkel. Ik mis de bon in mijn tas. Kunt u een kopie mailen?

Met vriendelijke groet,
[jouw naam]

**Notice:** polite opening, short problem, clear request, closing.

**Later you will write:** Schrijf 5 zinnen over je buurt: wat vind je fijn, wat wil je verbeteren?
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — *er* + place**

**Rule:** *Er is / er zijn* introduces existence; prepositions pin location (*in, op, naast*).

**Patterns:**
• *Er is een lift.* · *Er zijn twee fietsenstallingen.* · *De sleutel ligt op tafel.*

**Produce:**
1. Describe your home with *Er is …* / *Er zijn …*.
2. Add one place preposition (*naast de deur*).

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — v / f:** *vandaag, vier* — light *v*, not English *w*.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**prepositions of place (naast, boven, achter, tussen)**
• NL: Er is een kleine lek in de badkamer. / Er zijn twee fietsenstallingen bij het flatgebouw.
• EN: There is a small leak in the bathroom. / There are two bike sheds by the block.

**er + locative (er is, er zijn)**
• NL: Onze huur is inclusief servicekosten. / Jouw sleutel ligt op de keukentafel.
• EN: Our rent includes service charges. / Your key is on the kitchen table.

**possessive pronouns (mijn, jouw, zijn/haar, ons)**
• NL: De buren wonen boven ons appartement. / Ik bel de verhuurder over het plafond.
• EN: The neighbours live above our flat. / I'm calling the landlord about the ceiling.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Label the parts:** opening / problem / request / closing in the model email.

**2 — Gap-fill:** Met vriendelijke ______, [naam].

**3 — Politeness:** choose softer Dutch: **Ik wil** → **Ik zou graag willen** in one sentence.

**4 — Combine:** Link with **want**: *Ik bel morgen, want ik ben vandaag druk.*

**5 — Shorten:** remove three words without losing the message.

**6 — Checklist tick:** subject line clear? closing present?

**7 — Your details:** replace [naam] and add one real detail from your life.

**8 — Read aloud:** read your draft slowly.
---

**All four skills — finish this lesson with these four micro-tasks**

🎧 **Listening:** Use TTS on this Dutch line (slow, then closer to natural): *Er is een kleine lek in de badkamer.*

📖 **Reading:** Read silently, then say the **main idea in one English sentence**: *Er is een kleine lek in de badkamer.*

✍️ **Writing:** Write **one new Dutch sentence** that uses **huur** and fits *Er is / er zijn + place*.

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*

**Mini situation**

You get a short message that sounds a bit too direct in Dutch. Your job in the next steps is to choose a polite fix.

**Message (Dutch):**

We will upgrade the tone without changing the facts.

**Dutch culture insight**

**Waste sorting** colours differ by gemeente; wrong bags can mean the truck leaves them behind. When in doubt, ask *Welke kleur zak voor plastic hier?*

**Phrases — landlord & neighbours**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Ik wil een storing melden.* | I want to report a fault. |
| *Kunt u kijken naar het plafond?* | Could you look at the ceiling? |
| *Tot welke tijd is het stil in het portiek?* | Until what time is it quiet in the stairwell? |

**Mini dialogue**

**Buur:** Goedemorgen, ik hoorde water in de badkamer.
**Jij:** O nee, ik bel de verhuurder en stuur u een foto.
**Buur:** Dank je, ik ben tot vijf uur thuis.

**Say or write:** One polite line to report a small problem at home.

*Optional reflection:* In one English sentence, what feels similar or different where you live?

steps.activity:
**Culture snapshot — the Netherlands**

Flats often share a central staircase (portiek); quiet hours (rust) after 22:00 are common in building rules.

**Mini text (Dutch):**
In veel appartementen staat een huisregel: na 22:00 rust op de gang. Buren lossen kleine problemen vaak eerst met een kort gesprek.

*In many flats there is a rule: quiet in the corridor after 22:00. Neighbours often solve small issues with a short chat first.*
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — ui / eu / ij:** round lips for *huis — leeuw*; *mijn* is one smooth glide.

**Useful phrases for talking about customs**

• **In Nederland …** — In the Netherlands …
• **Bij ons …** — Where I'm from …
• **Mag ik vragen …?** — May I ask …?

**Context:** Flats often share a central staircase (portiek); quiet hours (rust) after 22:00 are common in building rules.

**Say or write:** one respectful question you could ask a Dutch friend about a tradition.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:

**4 — Gist:** what is **rust** on a building notice about?

**5 — Reflect:** one difference between your country and NL (English ok).

**6 — Gap-fill:** In Nederland fietsen veel mensen, _____ het is plat.

**7 — Scenario:** friend cancels last minute — what do many Dutch people appreciate?

**8 — Your question in Dutch:** **Mag ik vragen hoe …?** finish it.

**Culture reminder:** Flats often share a central staircase (portiek); quiet hours (rust) after 22:00 are common in building rules.

items.prompt: Pick the best reply when someone says **Hoe gaat het?**
items.feedbackCorrect: Nice — this is a natural, friendly reply.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Tot ziens** is goodbye; **Graag gedaan** fits after *dank je*. After *Hoe gaat het?* people often say how they feel, e.g. **Goed, dank je!**

items.prompt: Complete: **Ik ___ Nederlands sinds januari.**
items.hint: Present tense of *leren* (to learn), 1st person singular.
items.feedbackCorrect: Yes — *Ik leer Nederlands sinds januari.*
items.feedbackIncorrect: The present of *leren* (ik-form) is **leer**.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.
items.feedbackIncorrect: This is **true**: many yes/no questions start with the verb.

items.prompt: Say or write **one new sentence** using **dak**.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — possessives**

**Rule:** *mijn, jouw, zijn, haar, ons/onze, jullie* agree in a natural way with the following noun (*onze huur*).

**Patterns:**
• *Onze buren zijn rustig.* · *Jouw paspoort ligt in de la.*

**Produce:**
1. Two true sentences: *mijn …* and *onze / jullie …*.
2. Ask whose object it is: *Wiens … is dit?* (or *Van wie is …?*).

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — sch / sj:** *school* vs *sjabloon* — listen twice, then shadow.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**prepositions of place (naast, boven, achter, tussen)**
• NL: Er zijn twee fietsenstallingen bij het flatgebouw. / Er is een kleine lek in de badkamer.
• EN: There are two bike sheds by the block. / There is a small leak in the bathroom.

**er + locative (er is, er zijn)**
• NL: Jouw sleutel ligt op de keukentafel. / Onze huur is inclusief servicekosten.
• EN: Your key is on the kitchen table. / Our rent includes service charges.

**possessive pronouns (mijn, jouw, zijn/haar, ons)**
• NL: Ik bel de verhuurder over het plafond. / De buren wonen boven ons appartement.
• EN: I'm calling the landlord about the ceiling. / The neighbours live above our flat.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

**3 — Mini dialogue**
**A:** Hoe laat ben je klaar? **B:** Om half zes, denk ik.

**4 — Find the odd one**
Which word fits *least* with this unit: **parkeerplaats**, **broodje**, **trein**? Why?

**5 — True / false**
Say **waar** or **niet waar**: "In Dutch, the verb often moves when you ask a yes/no question."

**6 — Your sentence**
Write one true sentence about yourself using **huisregels** or **afvalscheiding**.

**7 — Transform**
Make this informal line a bit more formal: "Kun je me helpen?" → start with **Kunt u …**

**8 — Quick translation check**
"Er zijn twee fietsenstallingen bij het flatgebouw." — what does it mean in English (your own words)?

steps.activity:
Welcome! This lesson is built in small steps so you always know where you are.

**What you'll be able to do:** Understand short input about transport & city, notice useful chunks, and answer gist/detail questions

**Grammar thread today:** Motion + prepositions (naar, met, van … naar …) — you'll get explanations, examples, and production tasks in **Language focus** and **Guided practice**.

Take your time — you can repeat any step. Try to say at least one Dutch sentence out loud today.

steps.activity:
Let's wake up vocabulary about **Transport & city**.

Words you might use today:
• **OV-chipkaart**
• **inchecken**
• **uitchecken**
• **vertraging**
• **spoor**
• **perron**

**Your turn (1–2 short sentences, Dutch or mixed):**

If you only know a few words, that's fine — start there.
steps.teacher_notes: Accept partial Dutch; encourage gestures.
steps.example_response: Gisteren nam ik de bus naar het centrum en wisselde ik één keer over. Ik was een beetje te laat, maar ik stuurde een appje: *Sorry, ik ben zo daar.*

illustration.alt: Simple route from A to B with a transfer stop in the middle and OV label

steps.activity:
**Listen & read** this short conversation. Read twice: first for gist, then for details.

**Reiziger:** Excuseer, welke bus gaat naar het ziekenhuis?

**Chauffeur:** Lijn 28, perron B. U moet bij 'Centrum' overstappen.

**Reiziger:** Hoe duurt dat ongeveer?

**Chauffeur:** Met overstap een klein half uur. Vergeet niet in te checken.

**Gist check:** Who are the people, and what is the situation?
**Detail check:** Note one time phrase or place you recognise.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — motion + prepositions**

**Rule:** *naar* (towards), *met* (by/with), *van … naar …* for routes.

**Patterns:**
• *Ik ga met de bus naar het centrum.* · *We fietsen van huis naar het station.*

**Produce:**
1. Describe one real trip with *van … naar …*.
2. Add *met de trein / met de auto*.

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — stress:** stress content words: *Ik wil een afspraak maken* (not every syllable equal).

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**motion verbs + preposition (naar, met, van … naar …)**
• NL: Ik ga met de bus naar het station. / We reizen van Utrecht naar Amsterdam.
• EN: I'm taking the bus to the station. / We're travelling from Utrecht to Amsterdam.

**connectors: eerst, daarna, totdat (simple sequences)**
• NL: We gaan morgen naar het museum. / Ik ga vanavond uit eten met een vriend.
• EN: We're going to the museum tomorrow. / I'm going out for dinner with a friend tonight.

**future with gaan + infinitive for plans**
• NL: Als de trein vertraging heeft, stuur ik een appje. / Wanneer je incheckt, vergeet niet uit te checken.
• EN: If the train is delayed, I'll send a text. / When you check in, don't forget to check out.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.


**Culture tip:** Nationwide OV-chipkaart or contactless check-in/out is standard; forgetting to check out can charge a higher fare.
*How does this compare with where you live? One sentence in your own words.*
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Match** (say the pairs out loud)
• **OV-chipkaart** ↔ a word you connect with daily life
• **inchecken** ↔ a place or thing from this unit

**2 — Gap-fill**
Complete: Ik _____ om acht uur. *(opstaan → stem: Ik **sta** … **op**)*

**3 — Mini dialogue**
**A:** Hoe laat ben je klaar? **B:** Om half zes, denk ik.

**4 — Find the odd one**
Which word fits *least* with this unit: **uitchecken**, **broodje**, **trein**? Why?

**5 — True / false**
Say **waar** or **niet waar**: "In Dutch, the verb often moves when you ask a yes/no question."

**6 — Your sentence**
Write one true sentence about yourself using **OV-chipkaart** or **inchecken**.

**7 — Transform**
Make this informal line a bit more formal: "Kun je me helpen?" → start with **Kunt u …**

**8 — Quick translation check**
"Ik ga met de bus naar het station." — what does it mean in English (your own words)?
---

**All four skills — finish this lesson with these four micro-tasks**

🎧 **Listening:** Use TTS on this Dutch line (slow, then closer to natural): *Reiziger: Excuseer, welke bus gaat naar het ziekenhuis?*

📖 **Reading:** Read silently, then say the **main idea in one English sentence**: *Reiziger: Excuseer, welke bus gaat naar het ziekenhuis?*

✍️ **Writing:** Write **one new Dutch sentence** that uses **OV-chipkaart** and fits *Motion + prepositions (naar, met, van … naar …)*.

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*
steps.teacher_notes: Mix modalities; at least 6 tasks — all concrete above.

steps.activity:
**Phrase bank (use freely)**

**Your task:** Write or say **4–6 short sentences** in Dutch about this unit theme.
Rules: include **at least one question** and one connector (**en**, **maar**, or **want**).

Try each question, then tap **Check answer**. The correct answer stays hidden until you submit.

items.prompt: Pick the best reply when someone says **Hoe gaat het?**
items.feedbackCorrect: Nice — this is a natural, friendly reply.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Tot ziens** is goodbye; **Graag gedaan** fits after *dank je*. After *Hoe gaat het?* people often say how they feel, e.g. **Goed, dank je!**

items.prompt: Complete: **Ik ___ Nederlands sinds januari.**
items.hint: Present tense of *leren* (to learn), 1st person singular.
items.feedbackCorrect: Yes — *Ik leer Nederlands sinds januari.*
items.feedbackIncorrect: The present of *leren* (ik-form) is **leer**.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.
items.feedbackIncorrect: This is **true**: many yes/no questions start with the verb.

items.prompt: Say or write **one new sentence** using **OV-chipkaart**.
items.exampleAnswer: Example: *Ik wil graag OV-chipkaart met mijn nieuwe buren.* (adjust to your situation.)

steps.activity:
**Nice work.**

Next in this unit: **Listen & read · The voices you meet on an ordinary day** — we keep building **Transport & city** with a new focus.

**2-minute revision:** skim the phrase bank from step 6 once more.

When you're ready, open **Quiz** for a compact recap — or **Flashcards** for the lemmas.

**Dutch culture insight**

**OV-chipkaart** and contactless check-in/out are standard; forgetting *uitchecken* can cost extra. *Vertraging* announcements are part of life; *De trein rijdt niet* is worth recognising by ear.

**Phrases — OV & delays**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Excuseer, welke tram gaat naar …?* | Excuse me, which tram goes to …? |
| *Ik moet overstappen bij …* | I have to transfer at … |
| *Er is vertraging door werkzaamheden.* | There is a delay due to engineering works. |

**Mini dialogue**

**Reiziger:** Hoe laat vertrekt de volgende bus naar het ziekenhuis?
**Medewerker:** Over zes minuten, halte twee. Vergeet niet in te checken.

**Say or write:** Say you are late and will send an app using *Sorry, ik heb vertraging*.

*Optional reflection:* In one English sentence, what feels similar or different where you live?
steps.teacher_notes: Link to next lesson id in CMS when wired.

steps.activity:
Welcome! This lesson is built in small steps so you always know where you are.

**What you'll be able to do:** Understand short input about transport & city, notice useful chunks, and answer gist/detail questions

**Grammar thread today:** Future plans with gaan + infinitive — you'll get explanations, examples, and production tasks in **Language focus** and **Guided practice**.

Take your time — you can repeat any step. Try to say at least one Dutch sentence out loud today.
steps.teacher_notes: Keep under ~1 min; reassure anxious learners.

steps.activity:
Let's wake up vocabulary about **Transport & city**.

Words you might use today:
• **vertraging**
• **spoor**
• **perron**
• ** overstap**
• **fietsenstalling**
• **route**
• **kaartje**
• **dienstregeling**

**Your turn (1–2 short sentences, Dutch or mixed):**
**Wat deed je gisteren?** — *What did you do yesterday?*

If you only know a few words, that's fine — start there.
steps.teacher_notes: Accept partial Dutch; encourage gestures.
steps.example_response: Gisteren nam ik de bus naar het centrum en wisselde ik één keer over. Ik was een beetje te laat, maar ik stuurde een appje: *Sorry, ik ben zo daar.*

illustration.alt: Simple route from A to B with a transfer stop in the middle and OV label

steps.activity:
**Listen & read** this short conversation. Read twice: first for gist, then for details.

**Collega:** De trein heeft twintig minuten vertraging.

**Jij:** Oei. Stuur je een app als je later bent?

**Collega:** Ja, ik kom rond tien over half. Start jij zonder mij?

**Jij:** Geen probleem. We bewaren een plek.

**Gist check:** Who are the people, and what is the situation?
**Detail check:** Note one time phrase or place you recognise.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — future with *gaan***

**Rule:** *gaan + infinitive* plans near future: *Ik **ga** morgen **werken***.

**Patterns:**
• *We gaan zaterdag naar de markt.* · *Ga je vanavond mee?*

**Produce:**
1. Three plans for next week with *gaan*.
2. One negative: *Ik ga niet …* + reason with *want*.

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — rhythm:** short chunks: *van de — naar het — met een* — run them together softly.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**motion verbs + preposition (naar, met, van … naar …)**
• NL: We reizen van Utrecht naar Amsterdam. / Ik ga met de bus naar het station.
• EN: We're travelling from Utrecht to Amsterdam. / I'm taking the bus to the station.

**connectors: eerst, daarna, totdat (simple sequences)**
• NL: Ik ga vanavond uit eten met een vriend. / We gaan morgen naar het museum.
• EN: I'm going out for dinner with a friend tonight. / We're going to the museum tomorrow.

**future with gaan + infinitive for plans**
• NL: Wanneer je incheckt, vergeet niet uit te checken. / Als de trein vertraging heeft, stuur ik een appje.
• EN: When you check in, don't forget to check out. / If the train is delayed, I'll send a text.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**Input — patterns**

Below are sentences from this theme. They show word order you can copy.

**motion verbs + preposition (naar, met, van … naar …)**
• NL: Ik ga met de bus naar het station. / We reizen van Utrecht naar Amsterdam.
• EN: I'm taking the bus to the station. / We're travelling from Utrecht to Amsterdam.

**connectors: eerst, daarna, totdat (simple sequences)**
• NL: We gaan morgen naar het museum. / Ik ga vanavond uit eten met een vriend.
• EN: We're going to the museum tomorrow. / I'm going out for dinner with a friend tonight.

**future with gaan + infinitive for plans**
• NL: Als de trein vertraging heeft, stuur ik een appje. / Wanneer je incheckt, vergeet niet uit te checken.
• EN: If the train is delayed, I'll send a text. / When you check in, don't forget to check out.

Read them aloud once, slowly.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — *als* / *wanneer* (simple)**

**Rule:** Keep the first clause manageable: *Als ik laat ben, …* — main clause follows familiar word order.

**Patterns:**
• *Als het regent, blijf ik thuis.* · *Wanneer je klaar bent, stuur je een appje.*

**Produce:**
1. One *Als …, …* sentence about transport or work.
2. One *Wanneer …* sentence (keep both parts short).

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — final -n:** *eten, gaan* — don’t swallow the *-n* completely in careful speech.

**Pronunciation spotlight — ui / eu / ij**

Repeat: **huis — blauw — mijn — leeuw — reis**.
Keep **ij** as one glide; round lips a little for **ui/eu**.



**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**motion verbs + preposition (naar, met, van … naar …)**
• NL: Ik ga met de bus naar het station. / We reizen van Utrecht naar Amsterdam.
• EN: I'm taking the bus to the station. / We're travelling from Utrecht to Amsterdam.

**connectors: eerst, daarna, totdat (simple sequences)**
• NL: We gaan morgen naar het museum. / Ik ga vanavond uit eten met een vriend.
• EN: We're going to the museum tomorrow. / I'm going out for dinner with a friend tonight.

**future with gaan + infinitive for plans**
• NL: Als de trein vertraging heeft, stuur ik een appje. / Wanneer je incheckt, vergeet niet uit te checken.
• EN: If the train is delayed, I'll send a text. / When you check in, don't forget to check out.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

**Reiziger:** Hoe laat vertrekt de volgende bus naar het ziekenhuis?

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — motion + prepositions**

**Rule:** *naar* (towards), *met* (by/with), *van … naar …* for routes.

**Patterns:**
• *Ik ga met de bus naar het centrum.* · *We fietsen van huis naar het station.*

**Produce:**
1. Describe one real trip with *van … naar …*.
2. Add *met de trein / met de auto*.

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — v / f:** *vandaag, vier* — light *v*, not English *w*.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**motion verbs + preposition (naar, met, van … naar …)**
• NL: We reizen van Utrecht naar Amsterdam. / Ik ga met de bus naar het station.
• EN: We're travelling from Utrecht to Amsterdam. / I'm taking the bus to the station.

**connectors: eerst, daarna, totdat (simple sequences)**
• NL: Ik ga vanavond uit eten met een vriend. / We gaan morgen naar het museum.
• EN: I'm going out for dinner with a friend tonight. / We're going to the museum tomorrow.

**future with gaan + infinitive for plans**
• NL: Wanneer je incheckt, vergeet niet uit te checken. / Als de trein vertraging heeft, stuur ik een appje.
• EN: When you check in, don't forget to check out. / If the train is delayed, I'll send a text.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Headline:** give this thread a title in English (max 6 words).

**2 — Times:** list every time or date you see.

**3 — Who must do what?** One sentence in English.

**4 — Gap-fill from text:** "De lift is morgen … voor onderhoud." *(from context)*

**5 — Problem / solution:** what is the problem and what is suggested?

**6 — Word guess:** what might **onderhoud** mean?

**7 — True / false:** "Everyone is angry in this chat."

**8 — Your reply:** write one short Dutch sentence you could post in this group.

**Text:**
[E-mail van sportvereniging]
Beste lid,
Training op dinsdag vervalt vanwege schoolactiviteiten in de hal. We trainen donderdag op hetzelfde tijdstip.
Groet,
Het bestuur

items.prompt: Pick the best reply when someone says **Hoe gaat het?**
items.feedbackCorrect: Nice — this is a natural, friendly reply.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Tot ziens** is goodbye; **Graag gedaan** fits after *dank je*. After *Hoe gaat het?* people often say how they feel, e.g. **Goed, dank je!**

items.prompt: Complete: **Ik ___ Nederlands sinds januari.**
items.hint: Present tense of *leren* (to learn), 1st person singular.
items.feedbackCorrect: Yes — *Ik leer Nederlands sinds januari.*
items.feedbackIncorrect: The present of *leren* (ik-form) is **leer**.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.
items.feedbackIncorrect: This is **true**: many yes/no questions start with the verb.

items.prompt: Say or write **one new sentence** using **kaartje**.
items.exampleAnswer: Example: *Ik wil graag kaartje met mijn nieuwe buren.* (adjust to your situation.)

**Dutch culture insight**

In cities, **parking and blue zones** (*betaald parkeren*) confuse almost everyone once. Reading the small sign (*ma–za 09:00–21:00*) saves fines.

**Phrases — OV & delays**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Excuseer, welke tram gaat naar …?* | Excuse me, which tram goes to …? |
| *Ik moet overstappen bij …* | I have to transfer at … |
| *Er is vertraging door werkzaamheden.* | There is a delay due to engineering works. |

**Mini dialogue**

**Reiziger:** Hoe laat vertrekt de volgende bus naar het ziekenhuis?
**Medewerker:** Over zes minuten, halte twee. Vergeet niet in te checken.

**Say or write:** Say you are late and will send an app using *Sorry, ik heb vertraging*.

steps.activity:
**Mini situation**

You get a short message that sounds a bit too direct in Dutch. Your job in the next steps is to choose a polite fix.

**Message (Dutch):**
OV, mijn check-in was fout. Los het op. Ik betaal niet dubbel.

**PT, my check-in was wrong. Fix it. I'm not paying double.**

We will upgrade the tone without changing the facts.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — *als* / *wanneer* (simple)**

**Rule:** Keep the first clause manageable: *Als ik laat ben, …* — main clause follows familiar word order.

**Patterns:**
• *Als het regent, blijf ik thuis.* · *Wanneer je klaar bent, stuur je een appje.*

**Produce:**
1. One *Als …, …* sentence about transport or work.
2. One *Wanneer …* sentence (keep both parts short).

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — ui / eu / ij:** round lips for *huis — leeuw*; *mijn* is one smooth glide.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**motion verbs + preposition (naar, met, van … naar …)**
• NL: We reizen van Utrecht naar Amsterdam. / Ik ga met de bus naar het station.
• EN: We're travelling from Utrecht to Amsterdam. / I'm taking the bus to the station.

**connectors: eerst, daarna, totdat (simple sequences)**
• NL: Ik ga vanavond uit eten met een vriend. / We gaan morgen naar het museum.
• EN: I'm going out for dinner with a friend tonight. / We're going to the museum tomorrow.

**future with gaan + infinitive for plans**
• NL: Wanneer je incheckt, vergeet niet uit te checken. / Als de trein vertraging heeft, stuur ik een appje.
• EN: When you check in, don't forget to check out. / If the train is delayed, I'll send a text.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.

steps.activity:
**1 — Tone:** which line feels too blunt for a landlord?

**2 — Rewrite opening** with **Goedemiddag** + **met vriendelijke groet** at the end.

**3 — Add one sentence** with the reason: *want de verwarming uitvalt bij kou.*

**5 — Word order:** fix: *Ik wil graag een afspraak morgen.*

**6 — Scenario match:** pick phrase: **Ik meld een storing.**

**7 — Read aloud** your improved message.

**8 — Check:** is the request clear in one glance?

items.prompt: Pick the best reply when someone says **Hoe gaat het?**
items.feedbackCorrect: Nice — this is a natural, friendly reply.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Tot ziens** is goodbye; **Graag gedaan** fits after *dank je*. After *Hoe gaat het?* people often say how they feel, e.g. **Goed, dank je!**

items.prompt: Complete: **Ik ___ Nederlands sinds januari.**
items.hint: Present tense of *leren* (to learn), 1st person singular.
items.feedbackCorrect: Yes — *Ik leer Nederlands sinds januari.*
items.feedbackIncorrect: The present of *leren* (ik-form) is **leer**.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.
items.feedbackIncorrect: This is **true**: many yes/no questions start with the verb.

items.prompt: Say or write **one new sentence** using **vertraging**.
items.exampleAnswer: Example: *Ik wil graag vertraging met mijn nieuwe buren.* (adjust to your situation.)

steps.activity:
**Nice work.**

**Dutch culture insight**

Cyclists have strong norms: **bellen**, *rechts houden*, and not stopping on a busy bike path. Pedestrian zones (*voetgangersgebied*) still have bikes sometimes, so stay alert.

**Phrases — OV & delays**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Excuseer, welke tram gaat naar …?* | Excuse me, which tram goes to …? |
| *Ik moet overstappen bij …* | I have to transfer at … |
| *Er is vertraging door werkzaamheden.* | There is a delay due to engineering works. |

**Mini dialogue**

**Reiziger:** Hoe laat vertrekt de volgende bus naar het ziekenhuis?
**Medewerker:** Over zes minuten, halte twee. Vergeet niet in te checken.

**Say or write:** Say you are late and will send an app using *Sorry, ik heb vertraging*.

*Optional reflection:* In one English sentence, what feels similar or different where you live?

steps.activity:
**Culture snapshot — the Netherlands**

Nationwide OV-chipkaart or contactless check-in/out is standard; forgetting to check out can charge a higher fare.

**Mini text (Dutch):**
In veel appartementen staat een huisregel: na 22:00 rust op de gang. Buren lossen kleine problemen vaak eerst met een kort gesprek.

*In many flats there is a rule: quiet in the corridor after 22:00. Neighbours often solve small issues with a short chat first.*
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — sch / sj:** *school* vs *sjabloon* — listen twice, then shadow.

**Useful phrases for talking about customs**

• **In Nederland …** — In the Netherlands …
• **Bij ons …** — Where I'm from …
• **Mag ik vragen …?** — May I ask …?

**Context:** Nationwide OV-chipkaart or contactless check-in/out is standard; forgetting to check out can charge a higher fare.

**Say or write:** one respectful question you could ask a Dutch friend about a tradition.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Match:** Koningsdag — orange / Sinterklaas — December gifts / kerst — family meal (discuss).

**2 — Institution:** where do you register an address? *(hint: **gemeente**)*

**3 — Sort:** "Direct can still be polite" — **waar / niet waar**?

**4 — Gist:** what is **rust** on a building notice about?

**5 — Reflect:** one difference between your country and NL (English ok).

**6 — Gap-fill:** In Nederland fietsen veel mensen, _____ het is plat.

**7 — Scenario:** friend cancels last minute — what do many Dutch people appreciate?

**8 — Your question in Dutch:** **Mag ik vragen hoe …?** finish it.

**Culture reminder:** Nationwide OV-chipkaart or contactless check-in/out is standard; forgetting to check out can charge a higher fare.

steps.activity:
**Mini situation**

You get a short message that sounds a bit too direct in Dutch. Your job in the next steps is to choose a polite fix.

**Message (Dutch):**
OV, mijn check-in was fout. Los het op. Ik betaal niet dubbel.

**PT, my check-in was wrong. Fix it. I'm not paying double.**

We will upgrade the tone without changing the facts.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — future with *gaan***

**Rule:** *gaan + infinitive* plans near future: *Ik **ga** morgen **werken***.

**Patterns:**
• *We gaan zaterdag naar de markt.* · *Ga je vanavond mee?*

**Produce:**
1. Three plans for next week with *gaan*.
2. One negative: *Ik ga niet …* + reason with *want*.

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — stress:** stress content words: *Ik wil een afspraak maken* (not every syllable equal).

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**motion verbs + preposition (naar, met, van … naar …)**
• NL: We reizen van Utrecht naar Amsterdam. / Ik ga met de bus naar het station.
• EN: We're travelling from Utrecht to Amsterdam. / I'm taking the bus to the station.

**connectors: eerst, daarna, totdat (simple sequences)**
• NL: Ik ga vanavond uit eten met een vriend. / We gaan morgen naar het museum.
• EN: I'm going out for dinner with a friend tonight. / We're going to the museum tomorrow.

**future with gaan + infinitive for plans**
• NL: Wanneer je incheckt, vergeet niet uit te checken. / Als de trein vertraging heeft, stuur ik een appje.
• EN: When you check in, don't forget to check out. / If the train is delayed, I'll send a text.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
Let's wake up vocabulary about **Health & body**.

Words you might use today:
• **huisarts**
• **spoed**
• **koorts**
• **hoofdpijn**
• **keel**
• **neus**
• **verkouden**
• **apotheek**

**Your turn (1–2 short sentences, Dutch or mixed):**
**Wat deed je gisteren?** — *What did you do yesterday?*

If you only know a few words, that's fine — start there.
steps.teacher_notes: Accept partial Dutch; encourage gestures.
steps.example_response: Gisteren voelde ik me niet zo goed en bleef ik thuis. Ik dronk veel thee en rustte 's middags even. 's Avonds ging het al wat beter.

steps.activity:
**Listen & read** this short conversation. Read twice: first for gist, then for details.

**Apotheker:** Goedemiddag. Waarmee kan ik helpen?

**Klant:** Ik heb hoofdpijn en een lichte koorts sinds gisteren.

**Apotheker:** Paracetamol is geschikt. Drink genoeg water en rust.

**Klant:** Moet ik naar de huisarts?

**Apotheker:** Bij hoge koorts of klachten langer dan drie dagen: bel uw huisarts.

**Gist check:** Who are the people, and what is the situation?
**Detail check:** Note one time phrase or place you recognise.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — reflexives (body & health)**

**Rule:** *zich* changes with subject (*ik voel **me***, *je wast **je***).

**Patterns:**
• *Ik voel me niet goed.* · *Ik heb me verkouden.* · *Wast je handen alsjeblieft.*

**Produce:**
1. Say how you feel with *Ik voel me …*.
2. One sentence for a simple hygiene / health habit.

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — rhythm:** short chunks: *van de — naar het — met een* — run them together softly.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**reflexive verbs (zich voelen, zich wassen)**
• NL: Ik voel me vandaag niet zo goed. / Je wast je handen voor het eten.
• EN: I don't feel so well today. / You wash your hands before eating.

**modal zullen for soft advice (Je zult moeten …) — recognition**
• NL: Gisteren had ik koorts en een zere keel. / Ik ben naar de huisartsenpraktijk gebeld.
• EN: Yesterday I had a fever and a sore throat. / I called the GP practice.

**past tense recognition (ik had pijn) for narratives**
• NL: De assistent vraagt of ik paracetamol verdraag. / Ik heb vandaag al drie liter water gedronken.
• EN: The assistant asks if I tolerate paracetamol. / I've already drunk three litres of water today.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.


**Culture tip:** The huisarts (GP) is the usual first contact; emergency line 112 is for life-threatening situations; pharmacies (apotheek) advise on dosage.
*How does this compare with where you live? One sentence in your own words.*
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Match** (say the pairs out loud)
• **huisarts** ↔ a word you connect with daily life
• **spoed** ↔ a place or thing from this unit

**2 — Gap-fill**
Complete: Ik _____ om acht uur. *(opstaan → stem: Ik **sta** … **op**)*

**3 — Mini dialogue**
**A:** Hoe laat ben je klaar? **B:** Om half zes, denk ik.

**4 — Find the odd one**
Which word fits *least* with this unit: **koorts**, **broodje**, **trein**? Why?

**5 — True / false**
Say **waar** or **niet waar**: "In Dutch, the verb often moves when you ask a yes/no question."

**6 — Your sentence**
Write one true sentence about yourself using **huisarts** or **spoed**.

**7 — Transform**
Make this informal line a bit more formal: "Kun je me helpen?" → start with **Kunt u …**

**8 — Quick translation check**
"Ik voel me vandaag niet zo goed." — what does it mean in English (your own words)?
---

**All four skills — finish this lesson with these four micro-tasks**

🎧 **Listening:** Use TTS on this Dutch line (slow, then closer to natural): *Apotheker: Goedemiddag. Waarmee kan ik helpen?*

📖 **Reading:** Read silently, then say the **main idea in one English sentence**: *Apotheker: Goedemiddag. Waarmee kan ik helpen?*

✍️ **Writing:** Write **one new Dutch sentence** that uses **huisarts** and fits *Reflexive verbs (zich voelen, zich wassen)*.

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*
steps.teacher_notes: Mix modalities; at least 6 tasks — all concrete above.

steps.activity:
**Phrase bank (use freely)**
• huisarts
• spoed
• koorts
• hoofdpijn
• keel
• neus
• verkouden
• apotheek
• recept
• paracetamol

**Your task:** Write or say **4–6 short sentences** in Dutch about this unit theme.
Rules: include **at least one question** and one connector (**en**, **maar**, or **want**).

**Example starter (you can change it):**
Vandaag … Ik wil … **want** … **En jij?**
steps.teacher_notes: Encourage intelligibility over perfection.

steps.activity:
**Quick self-check**

Try each question, then tap **Check answer**. The correct answer stays hidden until you submit.

**Tip:** Say Dutch options out loud if that helps.

**Afterwards:** If three or more felt easy, open **Quiz** for a short recap. If not, go back to **Guided practice** once more.

items.prompt: Pick the best reply when someone says **Hoe gaat het?**
items.feedbackCorrect: Nice — this is a natural, friendly reply.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Tot ziens** is goodbye; **Graag gedaan** fits after *dank je*. After *Hoe gaat het?* people often say how they feel, e.g. **Goed, dank je!**

items.prompt: Complete: **Ik ___ Nederlands sinds januari.**
items.hint: Present tense of *leren* (to learn), 1st person singular.
items.feedbackCorrect: Yes — *Ik leer Nederlands sinds januari.*
items.feedbackIncorrect: The present of *leren* (ik-form) is **leer**.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.
items.feedbackIncorrect: This is **true**: many yes/no questions start with the verb.

items.prompt: Say or write **one new sentence** using **huisarts**.
items.exampleAnswer: Example: *Ik wil graag huisarts met mijn nieuwe buren.* (adjust to your situation.)

steps.activity:
**Nice work.**

Next in this unit: **Listen & read · Ears and eyes on realistic turns** — we keep building **Health & body** with a new focus.

**2-minute revision:** skim the phrase bank from step 6 once more.

When you're ready, open **Quiz** for a compact recap — or **Flashcards** for the lemmas.

**Dutch culture insight**

The **huisarts** gatekeeps most care; for non-emergencies you often need that referral first. **112** is for life-threatening emergencies; *Huisartsenpost* handles urgent evenings/weekends in many regions.

**Phrases — GP & pharmacy**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Ik wil graag een afspraak maken.* | I'd like to make an appointment. |
| *Ik ben verkouden sinds maandag.* | I've had a cold since Monday. |
| *Hoe vaak moet ik deze pillen innemen?* | How often should I take these pills? |

**Mini dialogue**

**Assistent:** Huisartsenpraktijk Noord, goedemiddag.
**Patiënt:** Goedemiddag, ik heb last van mijn keel en lichte koorts.
**Assistent:** Dokter kan u om 16:10 zien. Neem uw zorgpas mee.

**Say or write:** Name one symptom + duration in Dutch.

*Optional reflection:* In one English sentence, what feels similar or different where you live?
steps.teacher_notes: Link to next lesson id in CMS when wired.

steps.activity:
**Listen & read** this short conversation. Read twice: first for gist, then for details.

**Assistent:** Huisartsenpost, goedenavond.

**Beller:** Mijn kind heeft koorts en wil niet eten.

**Assistent:** Hoe oud is uw kind en sinds wanneer?

**Beller:** Zes jaar, sinds vanmiddag. 38,5 graden.

**Assistent:** Bel uw eigen huisarts morgenochtend. Bij ademnood of bewusteloosheid: bel 112.

**Gist check:** Who are the people, and what is the situation?
**Detail check:** Note one time phrase or place you recognise.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — perfectum (short)**

**Rule:** *hebben/zijn + voltooid deelwoord* for completed experiences: *Ik **heb** gewerkt*, *Ik **ben** gekomen*.

**Patterns:**
• *Gisteren heb ik boodschappen gedaan.* · *Ben je op tijd gekomen?*

**Produce:**
1. One sentence: what you did yesterday (*gisteren heb ik …*).
2. One question with *heb je …* or *ben je …*.

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — final -n:** *eten, gaan* — don’t swallow the *-n* completely in careful speech.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**reflexive verbs (zich voelen, zich wassen)**
• NL: Je wast je handen voor het eten. / Ik voel me vandaag niet zo goed.
• EN: You wash your hands before eating. / I don't feel so well today.

**modal zullen for soft advice (Je zult moeten …) — recognition**
• NL: Ik ben naar de huisartsenpraktijk gebeld. / Gisteren had ik koorts en een zere keel.
• EN: I called the GP practice. / Yesterday I had a fever and a sore throat.

**past tense recognition (ik had pijn) for narratives**
• NL: Ik heb vandaag al drie liter water gedronken. / De assistent vraagt of ik paracetamol verdraag.
• EN: I've already drunk three litres of water today. / The assistant asks if I tolerate paracetamol.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

items.prompt: Pick the best reply when someone says **Hoe gaat het?**
items.feedbackCorrect: Nice — this is a natural, friendly reply.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Tot ziens** is goodbye; **Graag gedaan** fits after *dank je*. After *Hoe gaat het?* people often say how they feel, e.g. **Goed, dank je!**

items.prompt: Complete: **Ik ___ Nederlands sinds januari.**
items.hint: Present tense of *leren* (to learn), 1st person singular.
items.feedbackCorrect: Yes — *Ik leer Nederlands sinds januari.*
items.feedbackIncorrect: The present of *leren* (ik-form) is **leer**.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.

**Dutch culture insight**

Calling in sick (*ziek melden*) is usually short and factual: *Ik ben ziek vandaag* plus expected return if you know it. Long emotional explanations are less common than in some workplaces.

**Phrases — GP & pharmacy**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Ik wil graag een afspraak maken.* | I'd like to make an appointment. |
| *Ik ben verkouden sinds maandag.* | I've had a cold since Monday. |
| *Hoe vaak moet ik deze pillen innemen?* | How often should I take these pills? |

steps.activity:
**Listening focus**

Play the audio twice if you can (slow speed first is fine).

**Transcript** (read along on the second listen):

Assistent: Huisartsenpraktijk Noord, goedemorgen.
Patiënt: Goedemorgen, ik ben verkouden en mijn keel doet pijn sinds maandag.
Assistent: Heeft u koorts gemeten?
Patiënt: Gisteren 37,8. Ik hoest ook een beetje.
Assistent: Dokter kan u om 15:20 zien. Neem uw zorgpas mee.
Patiënt: Prima, dank u wel.

**While you listen:** underline in your mind who wants what and any numbers you hear.

---

**Script — huisarts phone (fragments)**

*Assistent:* Huisartsenpraktijk De Linde, goedemiddag.
*Patiënt:* Goedemiddag, ik wil graag een afspraak. Ik ben al drie dagen verkouden.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — perfectum (short)**

**Rule:** *hebben/zijn + voltooid deelwoord* for completed experiences: *Ik **heb** gewerkt*, *Ik **ben** gekomen*.

**Patterns:**
• *Gisteren heb ik boodschappen gedaan.* · *Ben je op tijd gekomen?*

**Produce:**
1. One sentence: what you did yesterday (*gisteren heb ik …*).
2. One question with *heb je …* or *ben je …*.

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — g / ch:** practise *goed — licht — dag — nacht* with TTS; back *g* vs softer *ch*.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**reflexive verbs (zich voelen, zich wassen)**
• NL: Je wast je handen voor het eten. / Ik voel me vandaag niet zo goed.
• EN: You wash your hands before eating. / I don't feel so well today.

**modal zullen for soft advice (Je zult moeten …) — recognition**
• NL: Ik ben naar de huisartsenpraktijk gebeld. / Gisteren had ik koorts en een zere keel.
• EN: I called the GP practice. / Yesterday I had a fever and a sore throat.

**past tense recognition (ik had pijn) for narratives**
• NL: Ik heb vandaag al drie liter water gedronken. / De assistent vraagt of ik paracetamol verdraag.
• EN: I've already drunk three litres of water today. / The assistant asks if I tolerate paracetamol.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:

**5 — Polite or informal:** find one **u**-sentence and one **je/jij**-sentence.

**6 — Paraphrase:** in English, one sentence — what do they want?

**7 — Shadowing:** read one speaker's lines aloud with the transcript.

**8 — New chunk:** pick one phrase to use in your own week.
