items.prompt: Pick the best reply when someone says **Hoe gaat het?**
items.feedbackCorrect: Nice — this is a natural, friendly reply.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Tot ziens** is goodbye; **Graag gedaan** fits after *dank je*. After *Hoe gaat het?* people often say how they feel, e.g. **Goed, dank je!**

items.prompt: Complete: **Ik ___ Nederlands sinds januari.**
items.hint: Present tense of *leren* (to learn), 1st person singular.
items.feedbackCorrect: Yes — *Ik leer Nederlands sinds januari.*
items.feedbackIncorrect: The present of *leren* (ik-form) is **leer**.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.
items.feedbackIncorrect: This is **true**: many yes/no questions start with the verb.

items.prompt: Say or write **one new sentence** using **festival**.
items.exampleAnswer: Example: *Ik wil graag festival met mijn nieuwe buren.* (adjust to your situation.)

**Mini dialogue**

**A:** Film om 20:00 — ga je mee?
**B:** Liever niet vanavond, ik ben moe. Volgende week wel?
**A:** Top, dan plannen we woensdag.

**Say or write:** Decline one invite politely with a short reason.

*Optional reflection:* In one English sentence, what feels similar or different where you live?

**Pronunciation — stress:** stress content words: *Ik wil een afspraak maken* (not every syllable equal).

• NL: Ik nodig je uit voor een borrel. / Zullen we zaterdag om half zeven afspreken?
• EN: I'm inviting you for drinks. / Shall we meet Saturday at half past six?

**Patterns:**
• *Ik blijf thuis, want ik ben ziek.* · *Ik blijf thuis, omdat ik ziek ben.*

**Produce:**
1. Same idea twice: once with *want*, once with *omdat* (keep it short).
2. Say which feels easier for you to hear in fast speech.

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — rhythm:** short chunks: *van de — naar het — met een* — run them together softly.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**want, omdat for reasons (simple clauses)**
• NL: Ik ga mee naar de film, want ik heb zin. / We blijven thuis, omdat het regent.
• EN: I'm coming to the film because I feel like it. / We're staying home because it's raining.

**graag, liever for preferences**
• NL: Ik ga liever met de fiets naar het station. / We drinken graag koffie op het terras.
• EN: I'd rather cycle to the station. / We like having coffee on the terrace.

**diminutives in informal speech (biertje, filmpje) — recognition/production**
• NL: Zullen we zaterdag om half zeven afspreken? / Ik nodig je uit voor een borrel.
• EN: Shall we meet Saturday at half past six? / I'm inviting you for drinks.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Choral read:** read role A, then B, then swap.

**2 — Slot-fill:** **A:** Mag ik …? **B:** **Natuurlijk**, …

**3 — Add one polite word** to each turn.

**4 — Branch:** B says "Helaas, ik kan niet." — how does A answer kindly?

**5 — Write three lines** at the gemeente desk (text only).

**6 — Opinion:** **Ik vind … leuk, want …** finish it.

**7 — Invite:** suggest a time with **Zullen we … om …?**

**8 — Self-check:** did you use **u** where needed?

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*

items.prompt: Pick the best reply when someone says **Hoe gaat het?**
items.feedbackCorrect: Nice — this is a natural, friendly reply.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Tot ziens** is goodbye; **Graag gedaan** fits after *dank je*. After *Hoe gaat het?* people often say how they feel, e.g. **Goed, dank je!**

items.prompt: Complete: **Ik ___ Nederlands sinds januari.**
items.hint: Present tense of *leren* (to learn), 1st person singular.
items.feedbackCorrect: Yes — *Ik leer Nederlands sinds januari.*
items.feedbackIncorrect: The present of *leren* (ik-form) is **leer**.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.
items.feedbackIncorrect: This is **true**: many yes/no questions start with the verb.

items.prompt: Say or write **one new sentence** using **uitnodigen**.
items.exampleAnswer: Example: *Ik wil graag uitnodigen met mijn nieuwe buren.* (adjust to your situation.)

steps.activity:
**Nice work.**

**Dutch culture insight**

**Netflix**, **NPO**, and **podcasts** mix with *omroep* talk. Subtitles (*ondertiteling*) help learners; many Dutch people watch English shows with Dutch subs.

**Phrases — invites & Dutch directness**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Zin om zaterdag iets te drinken?* | Fancy a drink on Saturday? |
| *Eerlijk gezegd, ik heb geen zin.* | Honestly, I don't feel like it. |
| *Geen probleem, tot een andere keer.* | No problem, another time. |

**Mini dialogue**

**A:** Film om 20:00 — ga je mee?
**B:** Liever niet vanavond, ik ben moe. Volgende week wel?
**A:** Top, dan plannen we woensdag.

**Say or write:** Decline one invite politely with a short reason.

steps.activity:
**Mini situation**

You get a short message that sounds a bit too direct in Dutch. Your job in the next steps is to choose a polite fix.

**Message (Dutch):**
Kom om zeven. Geen discussie. Ik bepaal.

**Come at seven. No discussion. I decide.**

We will upgrade the tone without changing the facts.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — *graag*, *liever*, opinions**

**Rule:** *Ik ga graag naar …* · *Ik ga liever met de fiets* — soft opinions without fighting.

**Patterns:**
• *Ik drink graag koffie.* · *Ik vind borrels gezellig.*

**Produce:**
1. *Ik ga liever … dan …* about weekend plans.
2. Invite someone with *Zin om …?* + time.

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — final -n:** *eten, gaan* — don’t swallow the *-n* completely in careful speech.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**want, omdat for reasons (simple clauses)**
• NL: We blijven thuis, omdat het regent. / Ik ga mee naar de film, want ik heb zin.
• EN: We're staying home because it's raining. / I'm coming to the film because I feel like it.

**graag, liever for preferences**
• NL: We drinken graag koffie op het terras. / Ik ga liever met de fiets naar het station.
• EN: We like having coffee on the terrace. / I'd rather cycle to the station.

**diminutives in informal speech (biertje, filmpje) — recognition/production**
• NL: Ik nodig je uit voor een borrel. / Zullen we zaterdag om half zeven afspreken?
• EN: I'm inviting you for drinks. / Shall we meet Saturday at half past six?

steps.activity:
**Culture snapshot — the Netherlands**

Splitting the bill (iedereen betaalt voor zich) is common among friends; birthdays are often celebrated with coffee and cake at work.

**Mini text (Dutch):**
In veel appartementen staat een huisregel: na 22:00 rust op de gang. Buren lossen kleine problemen vaak eerst met een kort gesprek.

*In many flats there is a rule: quiet in the corridor after 22:00. Neighbours often solve small issues with a short chat first.*
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — v / f:** *vandaag, vier* — light *v*, not English *w*.

**Useful phrases for talking about customs**

• **In Nederland …** — In the Netherlands …
• **Bij ons …** — Where I'm from …
• **Mag ik vragen …?** — May I ask …?

**Context:** Splitting the bill (iedereen betaalt voor zich) is common among friends; birthdays are often celebrated with coffee and cake at work.

**Say or write:** one respectful question you could ask a Dutch friend about a tradition.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Match:** Koningsdag — orange / Sinterklaas — December gifts / kerst — family meal (discuss).

**2 — Institution:** where do you register an address? *(hint: **gemeente**)*

**3 — Sort:** "Direct can still be polite" — **waar / niet waar**?

**4 — Gist:** what is **rust** on a building notice about?

**5 — Reflect:** one difference between your country and NL (English ok).

**6 — Gap-fill:** In Nederland fietsen veel mensen, _____ het is plat.

**7 — Scenario:** friend cancels last minute — what do many Dutch people appreciate?

**8 — Your question in Dutch:** **Mag ik vragen hoe …?** finish it.

**Culture reminder:** Splitting the bill (iedereen betaalt voor zich) is common among friends; birthdays are often celebrated with coffee and cake at work.
---

📖 **Reading:** Read silently, then say the **main idea in one English sentence**: *Ik ga mee naar de film, want ik heb zin.*

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*

steps.activity:
Welcome! This lesson is built in small steps so you always know where you are.

**What you'll be able to do:** Complete a small real-world task about social & leisure (fix a message, choose replies, short role-card)

**Grammar thread today:** Preferences: graag, liever, ik vind … leuk — you'll get explanations, examples, and production tasks in **Language focus** and **Guided practice**.

Take your time — you can repeat any step. Try to say at least one Dutch sentence out loud today.
steps.teacher_notes: Keep under ~1 min; reassure anxious learners.

steps.activity:
Let's wake up vocabulary about **Social & leisure**.

Words you might use today:
• **voorstel**
• **afspraak**
• **gezellig**
• **uitnodigen**
• **afzeggen**
• **sporten**
• **koor**
• **museum**

**Your turn (1–2 short sentences, Dutch or mixed):**
**Wat deed je gisteren?** — *What did you do yesterday?*

If you only know a few words, that's fine — start there.
steps.teacher_notes: Accept partial Dutch; encourage gestures.
steps.example_response: Gisteren keek ik een serie en las ik een artikel online. Later belde ik mijn zus en we spraken over het weekend.

steps.activity:
**Mini situation**

You get a short message that sounds a bit too direct in Dutch. Your job in the next steps is to choose a polite fix.

**Message (Dutch):**
Kom om zeven. Geen discussie. Ik bepaal.

**Come at seven. No discussion. I decide.**

We will upgrade the tone without changing the facts.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — *graag*, *liever*, opinions**

**Rule:** *Ik ga graag naar …* · *Ik ga liever met de fiets* — soft opinions without fighting.

**Patterns:**
• *Ik drink graag koffie.* · *Ik vind borrels gezellig.*

**Produce:**
1. *Ik ga liever … dan …* about weekend plans.
2. Invite someone with *Zin om …?* + time.

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — g / ch:** practise *goed — licht — dag — nacht* with TTS; back *g* vs softer *ch*.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**want, omdat for reasons (simple clauses)**
• NL: We blijven thuis, omdat het regent. / Ik ga mee naar de film, want ik heb zin.
• EN: We're staying home because it's raining. / I'm coming to the film because I feel like it.

**graag, liever for preferences**
• NL: We drinken graag koffie op het terras. / Ik ga liever met de fiets naar het station.
• EN: We like having coffee on the terrace. / I'd rather cycle to the station.

**diminutives in informal speech (biertje, filmpje) — recognition/production**
• NL: Ik nodig je uit voor een borrel. / Zullen we zaterdag om half zeven afspreken?
• EN: I'm inviting you for drinks. / Shall we meet Saturday at half past six?

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Tone:** which line feels too blunt for a landlord?

**2 — Rewrite opening** with **Goedemiddag** + **met vriendelijke groet** at the end.

**3 — Add one sentence** with the reason: *want de verwarming uitvalt bij kou.*

**4 — Choose best closing:** A) Groetjes B) Met vriendelijke groet C) Doei

**5 — Word order:** fix: *Ik wil graag een afspraak morgen.*

**6 — Scenario match:** pick phrase: **Ik meld een storing.**

**7 — Read aloud** your improved message.

**8 — Check:** is the request clear in one glance?
---

**All four skills — finish this lesson with these four micro-tasks**

🎧 **Listening:** Use TTS on this Dutch line (slow, then closer to natural): *Kom om zeven. Geen discussie. Ik bepaal.*

📖 **Reading:** Read silently, then say the **main idea in one English sentence**: *Kom om zeven. Geen discussie. Ik bepaal.*

✍️ **Writing:** Write **one new Dutch sentence** that uses **voorstel** and fits *Preferences: graag, liever, ik vind … leuk*.

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*
steps.teacher_notes: Mix modalities; at least 6 tasks — all concrete above.

steps.activity:
**Phrase bank (use freely)**
• voorstel
• afspraak
• gezellig
• uitnodigen
• afzeggen
• sporten
• koor
• museum
• bioscoop
• festival

**Your task:** Write or say **4–6 short sentences** in Dutch about this unit theme.
Rules: include **at least one question** and one connector (**en**, **maar**, or **want**).

**Example starter (you can change it):**
Vandaag … Ik wil … **want** … **En jij?**
steps.teacher_notes: Encourage intelligibility over perfection.

steps.activity:
**Quick self-check**

Try each question, then tap **Check answer**. The correct answer stays hidden until you submit.

**Tip:** Say Dutch options out loud if that helps.

**Afterwards:** If three or more felt easy, open **Quiz** for a short recap. If not, go back to **Guided practice** once more.
steps.teacher_notes: Rendered with interactive UI when interaction is present.

items.prompt: Pick the best reply when someone says **Hoe gaat het?**
items.feedbackCorrect: Nice — this is a natural, friendly reply.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Tot ziens** is goodbye; **Graag gedaan** fits after *dank je*. After *Hoe gaat het?* people often say how they feel, e.g. **Goed, dank je!**

items.prompt: Complete: **Ik ___ Nederlands sinds januari.**
items.hint: Present tense of *leren* (to learn), 1st person singular.
items.feedbackCorrect: Yes — *Ik leer Nederlands sinds januari.*
items.feedbackIncorrect: The present of *leren* (ik-form) is **leer**.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.
items.feedbackIncorrect: This is **true**: many yes/no questions start with the verb.

items.prompt: Say or write **one new sentence** using **voorstel**.
items.exampleAnswer: Example: *Ik wil graag voorstel met mijn nieuwe buren.* (adjust to your situation.)

steps.activity:
**Nice work.**

Next unit starts with **Listen & read · Catch real Dutch in short, friendly chats**. You can preview the new words in Browse whenever you like.

**2-minute revision:** skim the phrase bank from step 6 once more.

When you're ready, open **Quiz** for a compact recap — or **Flashcards** for the lemmas.

**Dutch culture insight**

Weekend plans often include **terras**, **festival**, or *gewoon thuis*. *Zin om …?* is the casual invite you will hear constantly.

**Phrases — invites & Dutch directness**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Zin om zaterdag iets te drinken?* | Fancy a drink on Saturday? |
| *Eerlijk gezegd, ik heb geen zin.* | Honestly, I don't feel like it. |
| *Geen probleem, tot een andere keer.* | No problem, another time. |

**Mini dialogue**

**A:** Film om 20:00 — ga je mee?
**B:** Liever niet vanavond, ik ben moe. Volgende week wel?
**A:** Top, dan plannen we woensdag.

**Say or write:** Decline one invite politely with a short reason.

*Optional reflection:* In one English sentence, what feels similar or different where you live?
steps.teacher_notes: Link to next lesson id in CMS when wired.

quiz_ideas: Self-check: choose the best reply to "Hoe gaat het?" (see step 7 in the lesson).
Gap-fill idea: write a sentence with **voorstel** in the present tense.
True/false: in Dutch yes/no questions the verb often comes before the subject.
Say one new polite request starting with **Kunt u …** or **Mag ik …**.
Paraphrase one Dutch line from the lesson input in your own English.
assessment.success_criteria: You can complete the step-7 self-check comfortably and produce at least one clear Dutch sentence about the lesson theme (spoken or written).

**What you'll be able to do:** Understand short input about culture & integration context, notice useful chunks, and answer gist/detail questions

steps.teacher_notes: Keep under ~1 min; reassure anxious learners.

steps.activity:
Let's wake up vocabulary about **Culture & integration context**.

Words you might use today:
• **Koningsdag**
• **Sinterklaas**
• **kerst**
• **oud en nieuw**
• **basisschool**
• **ouderspreekuur**
• **directheid**
• **fietsen**

**Your turn (1–2 short sentences, Dutch or mixed):**
**Wat deed je gisteren?** — *What did you do yesterday?*

If you only know a few words, that's fine — start there.
steps.teacher_notes: Accept partial Dutch; encourage gestures.
steps.example_response: Gisteren sprak ik met een vriend over **Koningsdag** en Nederlandse tradities. We dronken koffie en wandelden een half uur door de buurt.

steps.activity:
**Listen & read** this short conversation. Read twice: first for gist, then for details.

**Gids:** Op Koningsdag dragen veel mensen oranje kleding.

**Toerist:** Wat gebeurt er op straat?

**Gids:** Kinderen verkopen spullen op de vrijmarkt. Er is muziek en soms een optocht.

**Toerist:** Moet ik contant geld meenemen?

**Gids:** Handig voor kleine aankopen. Veel kraampjes accepteren ook Tikkie.

**Gist check:** Who are the people, and what is the situation?
**Detail check:** Note one time phrase or place you recognise.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — culture chunks**

**Rule:** Fixed phrases beat abstract essays: *Mag ik vragen …?* · *Bij ons …* · *Hoe vieren jullie …?*

**Patterns:**
• *Eerlijk gezegd …* · *Ik wil geen gedoe maken, maar …*

**Produce:**
1. Ask one respectful question about a Dutch tradition.
2. Compare with *Bij ons is dat anders, want …* (short).

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — ui / eu / ij:** round lips for *huis — leeuw*; *mijn* is one smooth glide.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**impersonal constructions (men …, je … as 'one')**
• NL: Mag ik vragen hoe jullie Koningsdag vieren? / In Nederland dragen sommige mensen oranje.
• EN: May I ask how you celebrate King's Day? / In the Netherlands some people wear orange.

**present tense narratives about customs**
• NL: Je spreekt hier vaak rustig aan bij de balie. / Men plant sociale afspraken vaak een dag van tevoren.
• EN: Here people often address the desk calmly. / People often plan social meetups a day ahead.

**fixed expressions for holidays and greetings**
• NL: Fijne feestdagen en tot volgend jaar! / Integratie is taal én gewoontes leren.
• EN: Happy holidays and see you next year! / Integration is language and learning habits.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.


**Culture tip:** This unit foregrounds situated cultural knowledge; every lesson links language to real Netherlands contexts.
*How does this compare with where you live? One sentence in your own words.*
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Match** (say the pairs out loud)
• **Koningsdag** ↔ a word you connect with daily life
• **Sinterklaas** ↔ a place or thing from this unit

**2 — Gap-fill**
Complete: Ik _____ om acht uur. *(opstaan → stem: Ik **sta** … **op**)*

**3 — Mini dialogue**
**A:** Hoe laat ben je klaar? **B:** Om half zes, denk ik.

**4 — Find the odd one**
Which word fits *least* with this unit: **kerst**, **broodje**, **trein**? Why?

**5 — True / false**
Say **waar** or **niet waar**: "In Dutch, the verb often moves when you ask a yes/no question."

**6 — Your sentence**
Write one true sentence about yourself using **Koningsdag** or **Sinterklaas**.

**7 — Transform**
Make this informal line a bit more formal: "Kun je me helpen?" → start with **Kunt u …**

**8 — Quick translation check**
"Mag ik vragen hoe jullie Koningsdag vieren?" — what does it mean in English (your own words)?
---

**All four skills — finish this lesson with these four micro-tasks**

🎧 **Listening:** Use TTS on this Dutch line (slow, then closer to natural): *Gids: Op Koningsdag dragen veel mensen oranje kleding.*

📖 **Reading:** Read silently, then say the **main idea in one English sentence**: *Gids: Op Koningsdag dragen veel mensen oranje kleding.*

✍️ **Writing:** Write **one new Dutch sentence** that uses **Koningsdag** and fits *Fixed phrases for traditions and comparison*.

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*

steps.activity:
**Nice work.**

Next in this unit: **Listen & read · Who said what? Listen, read, respond** — we keep building **Culture & integration context** with a new focus.

**2-minute revision:** skim the phrase bank from step 6 once more.

When you're ready, open **Quiz** for a compact recap — or **Flashcards** for the lemmas.

**Dutch culture insight**

**Koningsdag** orange, **Sinterklaas** debates, and **kerst** traditions differ by family; curiosity beats assumptions. *Hoe vieren jullie …?* is a respectful opener.

**Phrases — school & integration tone**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Mag ik een afspraak met de leerkracht?* | May I have an appointment with the teacher? |
| *Mijn kind heeft moeite met huiswerk.* | My child struggles with homework. |
| *Hoe meld ik mijn kind ziek?* | How do I report my child sick? |

**Mini dialogue**

**Ouder:** Goedemiddag, ik bel over de ouderavond volgende week.
**School:** Zeker. De avond begint om 19:30; u krijgt een link per e-mail.

**Say or write:** Ask one respectful question about a Dutch tradition with *Mag ik vragen …?*

*Optional reflection:* In one English sentence, what feels similar or different where you live?

**Ouder:** Op de basisschool krijgen we berichten via de ouderapp.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — general statements**

• *In Nederland fietsen veel mensen.* · *Je spreekt hier rustig aan bij de balie.*

**Produce:**

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — sch / sj:** *school* vs *sjabloon* — listen twice, then shadow.

• NL: Integratie is taal én gewoontes leren. / Fijne feestdagen en tot volgend jaar!
• EN: Integration is language and learning habits. / Happy holidays and see you next year!

items.prompt: Pick the best reply when someone says **Hoe gaat het?**
items.feedbackCorrect: Nice — this is a natural, friendly reply.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Tot ziens** is goodbye; **Graag gedaan** fits after *dank je*. After *Hoe gaat het?* people often say how they feel, e.g. **Goed, dank je!**

items.prompt: Complete: **Ik ___ Nederlands sinds januari.**
items.hint: Present tense of *leren* (to learn), 1st person singular.
items.feedbackCorrect: Yes — *Ik leer Nederlands sinds januari.*
items.feedbackIncorrect: The present of *leren* (ik-form) is **leer**.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.
items.feedbackIncorrect: This is **true**: many yes/no questions start with the verb.

items.prompt: Say or write **one new sentence** using **oud en nieuw**.
items.exampleAnswer: Example: *Ik wil graag oud en nieuw met mijn nieuwe buren.* (adjust to your situation.)

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — culture chunks**

**Rule:** Fixed phrases beat abstract essays: *Mag ik vragen …?* · *Bij ons …* · *Hoe vieren jullie …?*

**Patterns:**
• *Eerlijk gezegd …* · *Ik wil geen gedoe maken, maar …*

**Produce:**
1. Ask one respectful question about a Dutch tradition.
2. Compare with *Bij ons is dat anders, want …* (short).

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — stress:** stress content words: *Ik wil een afspraak maken* (not every syllable equal).

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**impersonal constructions (men …, je … as 'one')**
• NL: Mag ik vragen hoe jullie Koningsdag vieren? / In Nederland dragen sommige mensen oranje.
• EN: May I ask how you celebrate King's Day? / In the Netherlands some people wear orange.

**present tense narratives about customs**
• NL: Je spreekt hier vaak rustig aan bij de balie. / Men plant sociale afspraken vaak een dag van tevoren.
• EN: Here people often address the desk calmly. / People often plan social meetups a day ahead.

**fixed expressions for holidays and greetings**
• NL: Fijne feestdagen en tot volgend jaar! / Integratie is taal én gewoontes leren.
• EN: Happy holidays and see you next year! / Integration is language and learning habits.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.


**Culture tip:** This unit foregrounds situated cultural knowledge; every lesson links language to real Netherlands contexts.
*How does this compare with where you live? One sentence in your own words.*

steps.activity:
**1 — Match** (say the pairs out loud)
• **directheid** ↔ a word you connect with daily life
• **fietsen** ↔ a place or thing from this unit

**2 — Gap-fill**
Complete: Ik _____ om acht uur. *(opstaan → stem: Ik **sta** … **op**)*

**3 — Mini dialogue**
**A:** Hoe laat ben je klaar? **B:** Om half zes, denk ik.

**4 — Find the odd one**
Which word fits *least* with this unit: **agenda**, **broodje**, **trein**? Why?

**5 — True / false**
Say **waar** or **niet waar**: "In Dutch, the verb often moves when you ask a yes/no question."

**6 — Your sentence**
Write one true sentence about yourself using **directheid** or **fietsen**.

**7 — Transform**
Make this informal line a bit more formal: "Kun je me helpen?" → start with **Kunt u …**

**8 — Quick translation check**
"Mag ik vragen hoe jullie Koningsdag vieren?" — what does it mean in English (your own words)?

**Transcript** (read along on the second listen):

Presentator: Vandaag: typische gewoontes rond feestdagen in Nederland.
Gast: Op kerst eten veel families warm met familie. Sinterklaas is vooral voor kinderen in december.
Presentator: En nieuwjaar?
Gast: Om middernacht proosten mensen, soms met oliebollen. In grote steden is vuurwerk populair, maar regels verschillen per gemeente.
Presentator: Dank voor de uitleg!

**While you listen:** underline in your mind who wants what and any numbers you hear.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — general statements**

**Rule:** *In Nederland …* · *Veel mensen …* · *Je …* (one) — useful for culture talk without blaming.

**Patterns:**
• *In Nederland fietsen veel mensen.* · *Je spreekt hier rustig aan bij de balie.*

**Produce:**
1. One neutral sentence about a habit with *In Nederland …*.
2. One careful question: *Mag ik vragen of …?*

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — rhythm:** short chunks: *van de — naar het — met een* — run them together softly.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**impersonal constructions (men …, je … as 'one')**
• NL: In Nederland dragen sommige mensen oranje. / Mag ik vragen hoe jullie Koningsdag vieren?

**present tense narratives about customs**
• NL: Men plant sociale afspraken vaak een dag van tevoren. / Je spreekt hier vaak rustig aan bij de balie.
• EN: People often plan social meetups a day ahead. / Here people often address the desk calmly.

**fixed expressions for holidays and greetings**
• NL: Integratie is taal én gewoontes leren. / Fijne feestdagen en tot volgend jaar!
• EN: Integration is language and learning habits. / Happy holidays and see you next year!

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.

Gast: Om middernacht proosten mensen, soms met oliebollen. In grote steden is vuurwerk populair, maar regels verschillen per gemeente.
Presentator: Dank voor de uitleg!

steps.activity:
Welcome! This lesson is built in small steps so you always know where you are.

**What you'll be able to do:** Write a short practical message about culture & integration context using a model, gaps, and a final checklist

**Grammar thread today:** Fixed phrases for traditions and comparison — you'll get explanations, examples, and production tasks in **Language focus** and **Guided practice**.

Take your time — you can repeat any step. Try to say at least one Dutch sentence out loud today.
steps.teacher_notes: Keep under ~1 min; reassure anxious learners.

steps.activity:
Let's wake up vocabulary about **Culture & integration context**.

Words you might use today:
• **Koningsdag**
• **Sinterklaas**
• **kerst**
• **oud en nieuw**
• **basisschool**
• **ouderspreekuur**
• **directheid**
• **fietsen**

**Your turn (1–2 short sentences, Dutch or mixed):**
**Wat deed je gisteren?** — *What did you do yesterday?*

If you only know a few words, that's fine — start there.
steps.teacher_notes: Accept partial Dutch; encourage gestures.
steps.example_response: Gisteren sprak ik met een vriend over **Koningsdag** en Nederlandse tradities. We dronken koffie en wandelden een half uur door de buurt.

steps.activity:
**Model message (Dutch)**

Subject: Vraag over bon

Goedemiddag,

Gisteren heb ik boodschappen gedaan bij uw winkel. Ik mis de bon in mijn tas. Kunt u een kopie mailen?

Met vriendelijke groet,
[jouw naam]

**Notice:** polite opening, short problem, clear request, closing.

**Later you will write:** Schrijf 6 zinnen over een Nederlandse traditie die je interessant vindt en waarom.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — culture chunks**

**Rule:** Fixed phrases beat abstract essays: *Mag ik vragen …?* · *Bij ons …* · *Hoe vieren jullie …?*

**Patterns:**
• *Eerlijk gezegd …* · *Ik wil geen gedoe maken, maar …*

**Produce:**
1. Ask one respectful question about a Dutch tradition.
2. Compare with *Bij ons is dat anders, want …* (short).

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — final -n:** *eten, gaan* — don’t swallow the *-n* completely in careful speech.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**impersonal constructions (men …, je … as 'one')**
• NL: Mag ik vragen hoe jullie Koningsdag vieren? / In Nederland dragen sommige mensen oranje.
• EN: May I ask how you celebrate King's Day? / In the Netherlands some people wear orange.

**present tense narratives about customs**
• NL: Je spreekt hier vaak rustig aan bij de balie. / Men plant sociale afspraken vaak een dag van tevoren.
• EN: Here people often address the desk calmly. / People often plan social meetups a day ahead.

**fixed expressions for holidays and greetings**
• NL: Fijne feestdagen en tot volgend jaar! / Integratie is taal én gewoontes leren.
• EN: Happy holidays and see you next year! / Integration is language and learning habits.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.


**Culture tip:** This unit foregrounds situated cultural knowledge; every lesson links language to real Netherlands contexts.
*How does this compare with where you live? One sentence in your own words.*
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Label the parts:** opening / problem / request / closing in the model email.

**2 — Gap-fill:** Met vriendelijke ______, [naam].

**3 — Politeness:** choose softer Dutch: **Ik wil** → **Ik zou graag willen** in one sentence.

**4 — Combine:** Link with **want**: *Ik bel morgen, want ik ben vandaag druk.*

**5 — Shorten:** remove three words without losing the message.

**6 — Checklist tick:** subject line clear? closing present?

**7 — Your details:** replace [naam] and add one real detail from your life.

**8 — Read aloud:** read your draft slowly.
---

**All four skills — finish this lesson with these four micro-tasks**

🎧 **Listening:** Use TTS on this Dutch line (slow, then closer to natural): *Mag ik vragen hoe jullie Koningsdag vieren?*

📖 **Reading:** Read silently, then say the **main idea in one English sentence**: *Mag ik vragen hoe jullie Koningsdag vieren?*

✍️ **Writing:** Write **one new Dutch sentence** that uses **Koningsdag** and fits *Fixed phrases for traditions and comparison*.

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*
steps.teacher_notes: Mix modalities; at least 6 tasks — all concrete above.

steps.activity:
**Writing task**

Schrijf 6 zinnen over een Nederlandse traditie die je interessant vindt en waarom.

**Phrase bank:**
• Koningsdag
• Sinterklaas
• kerst
• oud en nieuw
• basisschool
• ouderspreekuur
• directheid
• fietsen
• agenda
• borrel
• traditie
• integratie

**Checklist before you finish:**
• Clear subject or first line
• Polite greeting
• Short paragraphs
• **Met vriendelijke groet** + name
steps.teacher_notes: Encourage intelligibility over perfection.

steps.activity:
**Quick self-check**

Try each question, then tap **Check answer**. The correct answer stays hidden until you submit.

**Tip:** Say Dutch options out loud if that helps.

**Afterwards:** If three or more felt easy, open **Quiz** for a short recap. If not, go back to **Guided practice** once more.
steps.teacher_notes: Rendered with interactive UI when interaction is present.

items.prompt: Pick the best reply when someone says **Hoe gaat het?**
items.feedbackCorrect: Nice — this is a natural, friendly reply.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Tot ziens** is goodbye; **Graag gedaan** fits after *dank je*. After *Hoe gaat het?* people often say how they feel, e.g. **Goed, dank je!**

items.prompt: Complete: **Ik ___ Nederlands sinds januari.**
items.hint: Present tense of *leren* (to learn), 1st person singular.
items.feedbackCorrect: Yes — *Ik leer Nederlands sinds januari.*
items.feedbackIncorrect: The present of *leren* (ik-form) is **leer**.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.
items.feedbackIncorrect: This is **true**: many yes/no questions start with the verb.

items.prompt: Say or write **one new sentence** using **Koningsdag**.
items.exampleAnswer: Example: *Ik wil graag Koningsdag met mijn nieuwe buren.* (adjust to your situation.)

steps.activity:
**Nice work.**

Next in this unit: **Real-life task · When manners matter as much as grammar** — we keep building **Culture & integration context** with a new focus.

**2-minute revision:** skim the phrase bank from step 6 once more.

When you're ready, open **Quiz** for a compact recap — or **Flashcards** for the lemmas.

**Dutch culture insight**

**Koningsdag** orange, **Sinterklaas** debates, and **kerst** traditions differ by family; curiosity beats assumptions. *Hoe vieren jullie …?* is a respectful opener.

**Phrases — school & integration tone**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Mag ik een afspraak met de leerkracht?* | May I have an appointment with the teacher? |
| *Mijn kind heeft moeite met huiswerk.* | My child struggles with homework. |
| *Hoe meld ik mijn kind ziek?* | How do I report my child sick? |

**Mini dialogue**

**Ouder:** Goedemiddag, ik bel over de ouderavond volgende week.
**School:** Zeker. De avond begint om 19:30; u krijgt een link per e-mail.

**Say or write:** Ask one respectful question about a Dutch tradition with *Mag ik vragen …?*

*Optional reflection:* In one English sentence, what feels similar or different where you live?
steps.teacher_notes: Link to next lesson id in CMS when wired.

quiz_ideas: Self-check: choose the best reply to "Hoe gaat het?" (see step 7 in the lesson).
Gap-fill idea: write a sentence with **Koningsdag** in the present tense.
True/false: in Dutch yes/no questions the verb often comes before the subject.
Say one new polite request starting with **Kunt u …** or **Mag ik …**.
Paraphrase one Dutch line from the lesson input in your own English.
assessment.success_criteria: You can complete the step-7 self-check comfortably and produce at least one clear Dutch sentence about the lesson theme (spoken or written).

steps.activity:
**Mini situation**

You get a short message that sounds a bit too direct in Dutch. Your job in the next steps is to choose a polite fix.

**Message (Dutch):**
Leg uit waarom Nederland altijd gelijk heeft. Snel.

**Explain why the Netherlands is always right. Quickly.**

We will upgrade the tone without changing the facts.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — general statements**

**Rule:** *In Nederland …* · *Veel mensen …* · *Je …* (one) — useful for culture talk without blaming.

**Patterns:**
• *In Nederland fietsen veel mensen.* · *Je spreekt hier rustig aan bij de balie.*

**Produce:**
1. One neutral sentence about a habit with *In Nederland …*.
2. One careful question: *Mag ik vragen of …?*

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — v / f:** *vandaag, vier* — light *v*, not English *w*.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**impersonal constructions (men …, je … as 'one')**
• NL: In Nederland dragen sommige mensen oranje. / Mag ik vragen hoe jullie Koningsdag vieren?
• EN: In the Netherlands some people wear orange. / May I ask how you celebrate King's Day?

**present tense narratives about customs**
• NL: Men plant sociale afspraken vaak een dag van tevoren. / Je spreekt hier vaak rustig aan bij de balie.
• EN: People often plan social meetups a day ahead. / Here people often address the desk calmly.

**fixed expressions for holidays and greetings**
• NL: Integratie is taal én gewoontes leren. / Fijne feestdagen en tot volgend jaar!
• EN: Integration is language and learning habits. / Happy holidays and see you next year!

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.


**Culture tip:** This unit foregrounds situated cultural knowledge; every lesson links language to real Netherlands contexts.
*How does this compare with where you live? One sentence in your own words.*

steps.activity:
**1 — Tone:** which line feels too blunt for a landlord?

**3 — Add one sentence** with the reason: *want de verwarming uitvalt bij kou.*

**4 — Choose best closing:** A) Groetjes B) Met vriendelijke groet C) Doei

**5 — Word order:** fix: *Ik wil graag een afspraak morgen.*

**6 — Scenario match:** pick phrase: **Ik meld een storing.**

**7 — Read aloud** your improved message.

**8 — Check:** is the request clear in one glance?

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*

steps.activity:
**Culture snapshot — the Netherlands**

This unit foregrounds situated cultural knowledge; every lesson links language to real Netherlands contexts.

**Mini text (Dutch):**
In veel appartementen staat een huisregel: na 22:00 rust op de gang. Buren lossen kleine problemen vaak eerst met een kort gesprek.

*In many flats there is a rule: quiet in the corridor after 22:00. Neighbours often solve small issues with a short chat first.*
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — g / ch:** practise *goed — licht — dag — nacht* with TTS; back *g* vs softer *ch*.

**Useful phrases for talking about customs**

• **In Nederland …** — In the Netherlands …
• **Bij ons …** — Where I'm from …
• **Mag ik vragen …?** — May I ask …?

**Context:** This unit foregrounds situated cultural knowledge; every lesson links language to real Netherlands contexts.

**Say or write:** one respectful question you could ask a Dutch friend about a tradition.
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Match:** Koningsdag — orange / Sinterklaas — December gifts / kerst — family meal (discuss).

**2 — Institution:** where do you register an address? *(hint: **gemeente**)*

**3 — Sort:** "Direct can still be polite" — **waar / niet waar**?

**4 — Gist:** what is **rust** on a building notice about?

**5 — Reflect:** one difference between your country and NL (English ok).

**6 — Gap-fill:** In Nederland fietsen veel mensen, _____ het is plat.

**7 — Scenario:** friend cancels last minute — what do many Dutch people appreciate?

**8 — Your question in Dutch:** **Mag ik vragen hoe …?** finish it.

**Culture reminder:** This unit foregrounds situated cultural knowledge; every lesson links language to real Netherlands contexts.

📖 **Reading:** Read silently, then say the **main idea in one English sentence**: *Mag ik vragen hoe jullie Koningsdag vieren?*

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

**Afterwards:** If three or more felt easy, open **Quiz** for a short recap. If not, go back to **Guided practice** once more.

items.prompt: Pick the best reply when someone says **Hoe gaat het?**
items.feedbackCorrect: Nice — this is a natural, friendly reply.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Tot ziens** is goodbye; **Graag gedaan** fits after *dank je*. After *Hoe gaat het?* people often say how they feel, e.g. **Goed, dank je!**

items.prompt: Complete: **Ik ___ Nederlands sinds januari.**
items.hint: Present tense of *leren* (to learn), 1st person singular.
items.feedbackCorrect: Yes — *Ik leer Nederlands sinds januari.*
items.feedbackIncorrect: The present of *leren* (ik-form) is **leer**.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.
items.feedbackIncorrect: This is **true**: many yes/no questions start with the verb.

items.prompt: Say or write **one new sentence** using **directheid**.
items.exampleAnswer: Example: *Ik wil graag directheid met mijn nieuwe buren.* (adjust to your situation.)

steps.activity:
**Nice work.**

Next in this unit: **Listen & read · Conversation routines you can reuse** — we keep building **Culture & integration context** with a new focus.

**2-minute revision:** skim the phrase bank from step 6 once more.

When you're ready, open **Quiz** for a compact recap — or **Flashcards** for the lemmas.

**Dutch culture insight**

**Borrel** culture mixes work and neighbours: a drink, bitterballen, and going home on time. *Ik moet de trein halen* is an accepted exit line.

**Phrases — school & integration tone**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Mag ik een afspraak met de leerkracht?* | May I have an appointment with the teacher? |
| *Mijn kind heeft moeite met huiswerk.* | My child struggles with homework. |
| *Hoe meld ik mijn kind ziek?* | How do I report my child sick? |

**Mini dialogue**

**Ouder:** Goedemiddag, ik bel over de ouderavond volgende week.
**School:** Zeker. De avond begint om 19:30; u krijgt een link per e-mail.

**Say or write:** Ask one respectful question about a Dutch tradition with *Mag ik vragen …?*

*Optional reflection:* In one English sentence, what feels similar or different where you live?

catalog.title: Listen & read · Conversation routines you can reuse
catalog.description: Conversation routines you can reuse · A2 listen & read in “Culture & integration context”: Dutch you can use the same week, guided steps, and a short check at the end.

pedagogy.objective: By the end of this lesson, you can understand short input about culture & integration context, notice useful chunks, and answer gist/detail questions
can_do_outcomes: Understand basics of primary school routines and parent contact (rough picture)
Describe one or two civic habits (cycling, calendars, punctuality) simply
Follow a short exchange in this theme and reuse one chunk.

grammar_points.point: impersonal constructions (men …, je … as 'one')
examples_nl: In Nederland dragen sommige mensen oranje.
Mag ik vragen hoe jullie Koningsdag vieren?
examples_en: In the Netherlands some people wear orange.
May I ask how you celebrate King's Day?

grammar_points.point: present tense narratives about customs
examples_nl: Men plant sociale afspraken vaak een dag van tevoren.
Je spreekt hier vaak rustig aan bij de balie.
examples_en: People often plan social meetups a day ahead.
Here people often address the desk calmly.

examples_nl: Integratie is taal én gewoontes leren.

examples_en: Integration is language and learning habits.

steps.activity:
Welcome! This lesson is built in small steps so you always know where you are.

**What you'll be able to do:** Understand short input about culture & integration context, notice useful chunks, and answer gist/detail questions

**Grammar thread today:** General statements (men / je / veel mensen …) — you'll get explanations, examples, and production tasks in **Language focus** and **Guided practice**.

Take your time — you can repeat any step. Try to say at least one Dutch sentence out loud today.
steps.teacher_notes: Keep under ~1 min; reassure anxious learners.

steps.activity:
Let's wake up vocabulary about **Culture & integration context**.

Words you might use today:
• **borrel**
• **traditie**
• **integratie**
• **Koningsdag**
• **Sinterklaas**
• **kerst**
• **oud en nieuw**
• **basisschool**

**Your turn (1–2 short sentences, Dutch or mixed):**
**Wat deed je gisteren?** — *What did you do yesterday?*

If you only know a few words, that's fine — start there.
steps.teacher_notes: Accept partial Dutch; encourage gestures.
steps.example_response: Gisteren sprak ik met een vriend over **Koningsdag** en Nederlandse tradities. We dronken koffie en wandelden een half uur door de buurt.

steps.activity:
**Listen & read** this short conversation. Read twice: first for gist, then for details.

**Trainer:** Nederlanders plannen graag. Een appje van tevoren is normaal.

**Deelnemer:** En als ik iets last-minute afzeg?

**Trainer:** Geef het zo snel mogelijk door. 'Sorry, het lukt niet' is oké.

**Gist check:** Who are the people, and what is the situation?
**Detail check:** Note one time phrase or place you recognise.
steps.teacher_notes: Optional slow audio for D/A.

steps.activity:
**Today's grammar — general statements**

**Rule:** *In Nederland …* · *Veel mensen …* · *Je …* (one) — useful for culture talk without blaming.

**Patterns:**
• *In Nederland fietsen veel mensen.* · *Je spreekt hier rustig aan bij de balie.*

**Produce:**
1. One neutral sentence about a habit with *In Nederland …*.
2. One careful question: *Mag ik vragen of …?*

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — ui / eu / ij:** round lips for *huis — leeuw*; *mijn* is one smooth glide.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**impersonal constructions (men …, je … as 'one')**
• NL: In Nederland dragen sommige mensen oranje. / Mag ik vragen hoe jullie Koningsdag vieren?
• EN: In the Netherlands some people wear orange. / May I ask how you celebrate King's Day?

**present tense narratives about customs**
• NL: Men plant sociale afspraken vaak een dag van tevoren. / Je spreekt hier vaak rustig aan bij de balie.
• EN: People often plan social meetups a day ahead. / Here people often address the desk calmly.

**fixed expressions for holidays and greetings**
• NL: Integratie is taal én gewoontes leren. / Fijne feestdagen en tot volgend jaar!
• EN: Integration is language and learning habits. / Happy holidays and see you next year!

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.


**Culture tip:** This unit foregrounds situated cultural knowledge; every lesson links language to real Netherlands contexts.
*How does this compare with where you live? One sentence in your own words.*
steps.teacher_notes: Max 2–3 patterns at A2.

steps.activity:
**1 — Match** (say the pairs out loud)
• **borrel** ↔ a word you connect with daily life
• **traditie** ↔ a place or thing from this unit

**2 — Gap-fill**
Complete: Ik _____ om acht uur. *(opstaan → stem: Ik **sta** … **op**)*

**3 — Mini dialogue**
**A:** Hoe laat ben je klaar? **B:** Om half zes, denk ik.

**4 — Find the odd one**
Which word fits *least* with this unit: **integratie**, **broodje**, **trein**? Why?

**5 — True / false**
Say **waar** or **niet waar**: "In Dutch, the verb often moves when you ask a yes/no question."

**6 — Your sentence**
Write one true sentence about yourself using **borrel** or **traditie**.

**7 — Transform**
Make this informal line a bit more formal: "Kun je me helpen?" → start with **Kunt u …**

**8 — Quick translation check**
"In Nederland dragen sommige mensen oranje." — what does it mean in English (your own words)?
---

**All four skills — finish this lesson with these four micro-tasks**

🎧 **Listening:** Use TTS on this Dutch line (slow, then closer to natural): *Trainer: Nederlanders plannen graag. Een appje van tevoren is normaal.*

📖 **Reading:** Read silently, then say the **main idea in one English sentence**: *Trainer: Nederlanders plannen graag. Een appje van tevoren is normaal.*

✍️ **Writing:** Write **one new Dutch sentence** that uses **borrel** and fits *General statements (men / je / veel mensen …)*.

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*
steps.teacher_notes: Mix modalities; at least 6 tasks — all concrete above.

steps.activity:
**Phrase bank (use freely)**
• borrel
• traditie
• integratie
• Koningsdag
• Sinterklaas
• kerst
• oud en nieuw
• basisschool
• ouderspreekuur
• directheid
• fietsen
• agenda

**Your task:** Write or say **4–6 short sentences** in Dutch about this unit theme.
Rules: include **at least one question** and one connector (**en**, **maar**, or **want**).

**Example starter (you can change it):**
Vandaag … Ik wil … **want** … **En jij?**
steps.teacher_notes: Encourage intelligibility over perfection.

**Nice work.**

You reached the end of this A2 path — huge win. Mix review, scenarios, and real-life practice from here.

**2-minute revision:** skim the phrase bank from step 6 once more.

When you're ready, open **Quiz** for a compact recap — or **Flashcards** for the lemmas.

**Dutch culture insight**

Integration courses stress both **language** and **local habits**: waste rules, GP registration, and school communication. Asking *Wat is hier normaal?* shows respect and buys patience.

**Phrases — school & integration tone**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Mag ik een afspraak met de leerkracht?* | May I have an appointment with the teacher? |
| *Mijn kind heeft moeite met huiswerk.* | My child struggles with homework. |
| *Hoe meld ik mijn kind ziek?* | How do I report my child sick? |

**Mini dialogue**

**Ouder:** Goedemiddag, ik bel over de ouderavond volgende week.
**School:** Zeker. De avond begint om 19:30; u krijgt een link per e-mail.

**Say or write:** Ask one respectful question about a Dutch tradition with *Mag ik vragen …?*

*Optional reflection:* In one English sentence, what feels similar or different where you live?
